เพลงชาติไทย *[Phleng Chat Thai](/wiki/Thai_National_Anthem "Thai National Anthem")* "Thai National Anthem"     **[Royal anthem](/wiki/Royal_anthem "Royal anthem"):** สรรเสริญพระบารมี *[Sansoen Phra Barami](/wiki/Sansoen_Phra_Barami "Sansoen Phra Barami")* "Praise the King" | |

until 1939,[[v]](#cite_note-siam-12) is a country located in [mainland Southeast Asia](/wiki/Mainland_Southeast_Asia "Mainland Southeast Asia"). It shares land borders with [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar") to the west and northwest, [Laos](/wiki/Laos "Laos") to the east and northeast, [Cambodia](/wiki/Cambodia "Cambodia") to the southeast, and [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia") to the south. Its maritime boundaries include the [Gulf of Thailand](/wiki/Gulf_of_Thailand "Gulf of Thailand") and the [Andaman Sea](/wiki/Andaman_Sea "Andaman Sea"), as well as maritime borders with [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), and [India](/wiki/India "India"). Thailand has a population of nearly 66 million people[[8]](#cite_note-13) and covers an area of approximately 513,115 km2 (198,115 sq mi).[[9]](#cite_note-14) The country's [capital](/wiki/Capital_city "Capital city") and [largest city](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Thailand#Largest_cities_by_urban_population "List of municipalities in Thailand") is [Bangkok](/wiki/Bangkok "Bangkok").[[10]](#cite_note-15)

**Thailand**,[[iii]](#cite_note-endo-10) officially the **Kingdom of Thailand**,[[iv]](#cite_note-11) and formerly known as **Siam** until 1939,[[v]](#cite_note-siam-12) is a country located in [mainland Southeast Asia](/wiki/Mainland_Southeast_Asia "Mainland Southeast Asia"). It shares land borders with [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar") to the west and northwest, [Laos](/wiki/Laos "Laos") to the east and northeast, [Cambodia](/wiki/Cambodia "Cambodia") to the southeast, and [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia") to the south. Its maritime boundaries include the [Gulf of Thailand](/wiki/Gulf_of_Thailand "Gulf of Thailand") and the [Andaman Sea](/wiki/Andaman_Sea "Andaman Sea"), as well as maritime borders with [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), and [India](/wiki/India "India"). Thailand has a population of nearly 66 million people[[8]](#cite_note-13) and covers an area of approximately 513,115 km2 (198,115 sq mi).[[9]](#cite_note-14) The country's [capital](/wiki/Capital_city "Capital city") and [largest city](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Thailand#Largest_cities_by_urban_population "List of municipalities in Thailand") is [Bangkok](/wiki/Bangkok "Bangkok").[[10]](#cite_note-15)

Archaeological evidence indicates that humans have inhabited the area of present-day Thailand for at least 40,000 years. Indigenous ethnic groups include the [Mon](/wiki/Mon_people "Mon people"), [Khmer](/wiki/Khmer_people "Khmer people"), and [Malay](/wiki/Malay_people "Malay people") people. The [Tai people](/wiki/Tai_people "Tai people") are believed to have originated from the [Điện Biên Phủ](/wiki/%C4%90i%E1%BB%87n_Bi%C3%AAn_Ph%E1%BB%A7 "Điện Biên Phủ") region since the 5th century and began migrating into the territory of modern Thailand between the 8th and 10th centuries. During the classical historical period, major kingdoms such as [Sukhothai](/wiki/Sukhothai_Kingdom "Sukhothai Kingdom"), [Lan Na](/wiki/Lan_Na "Lan Na"), and [Ayutthaya](/wiki/Ayutthaya_Kingdom "Ayutthaya Kingdom") were established. The Sukhothai Kingdom is regarded as the beginning of [Thai history](/wiki/Thai_history "Thai history"), while the Ayutthaya Kingdom, founded in 1350 CE, became a [regional power](/wiki/Regional_power "Regional power") replacing the [Khmer Empire](/wiki/Khmer_Empire "Khmer Empire"). European contact began in 1511 CE when [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire") envoys arrived in Ayutthaya. The Ayutthaya Kingdom flourished until its complete destruction during the [1765–1767 Burmese–Siamese War](/wiki/Burmese%E2%80%93Siamese_War_(1765%E2%80%931767) "Burmese–Siamese War (1765–1767)") by the [Burmese forces](/wiki/Royal_Burmese_Armed_Forces "Royal Burmese Armed Forces") under the [Konbaung dynasty](/wiki/Konbaung_dynasty "Konbaung dynasty") in 1767.

After the fall of Ayutthaya, [King Taksin](/wiki/King_Taksin "King Taksin") reunified the kingdom and established the [Thonburi Kingdom](/wiki/Thonburi_Kingdom "Thonburi Kingdom"), which lasted only 15 years before he was overthrown by [Phutthayotfa Chulalok](/wiki/Rama_I "Rama I") (Rama I), the founder of the [Chakri dynasty](/wiki/Chakri_dynasty "Chakri dynasty"). King Rama I established the [Rattanakosin Kingdom](/wiki/Rattanakosin_Kingdom "Rattanakosin Kingdom") and moved the capital to Bangkok in 1782. During the era of [Western imperialism](/wiki/Western_imperialism_in_Asia "Western imperialism in Asia"), Siam remained the only country in Southeast Asia to avoid colonisation by European powers, although it ceded territory, trade rights, and legal privileges through several [unequal treaties](/wiki/Unequal_treaties "Unequal treaties").[[11]](#cite_note-16) The governance system evolved into an [absolute monarchy](/wiki/Absolute_monarchy "Absolute monarchy") centralised under the rule of [King Chulalongkorn](/wiki/King_Chulalongkorn "King Chulalongkorn") (Rama V). Siam [adapted to international relations](/wiki/Chulalongkorn%27s_first_visit_to_Europe "Chulalongkorn's first visit to Europe") during the imperialist era and joined [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") on the side of the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I"), a political decision aimed at revising the effects of unequal treaties and enhancing Siam's international status.

Following the [Siamese revolution of 1932](/wiki/Siamese_revolution_of_1932 "Siamese revolution of 1932") by the [Khana Ratsadon](/wiki/Khana_Ratsadon "Khana Ratsadon") (lit. 'People's Party'), Siam transitioned from an absolute monarchy to a [parliamentary constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Parliamentary_constitutional_monarchy "Parliamentary constitutional monarchy") and was officially renamed Thailand. During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II_in_Thailand "World War II in Thailand"), the country was under the [military dictatorship](/wiki/Military_dictatorship "Military dictatorship") of [Plaek Phibunsongkhram](/wiki/Plaek_Phibunsongkhram "Plaek Phibunsongkhram") and allied with the [Empire of Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan") as part of the [Axis powers](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers"), but Thailand did not become a defeated nation due to the underground [Free Thai Movement](/wiki/Free_Thai_Movement "Free Thai Movement")'s "Declaration of Peace", which was recognised by the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_in_World_War_II "Allies in World War II"). During the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"), Thailand became a key [major non-NATO ally](/wiki/Major_non-NATO_ally "Major non-NATO ally") of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and played a major role in [countering communism](/wiki/Anti-communism "Anti-communism") in the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War"), the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"), and other [proxy wars](/wiki/Proxy_wars "Proxy wars"). Thailand also joined the [Southeast Asia Treaty Organization](/wiki/Southeast_Asia_Treaty_Organization "Southeast Asia Treaty Organization") (SEATO). Despite brief periods of [liberal democracy](/wiki/Liberal_democracy "Liberal democracy") in the 1970s and 1990s, Thailand alternated between [liberal democracy](/wiki/Liberal_democracy "Liberal democracy") and military dictatorship.

Since the 2000s, Thailand has experienced political conflict between supporters and opponents of Prime Minister [Thaksin Shinawatra](/wiki/Thaksin_Shinawatra "Thaksin Shinawatra") (in office [2001–2006](/wiki/Premiership_of_Thaksin_Shinawatra "Premiership of Thaksin Shinawatra")), leading to the [2006](/wiki/2006_Thai_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "2006 Thai coup d'état") and [2014](/wiki/2014_Thai_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "2014 Thai coup d'état") coup d'états. Today, Thailand operates under the [2017 Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Thailand "Constitution of Thailand") and a [coalition government](/wiki/Coalition_government "Coalition government") following the [2019 Thai general election](/wiki/2019_Thai_general_election "2019 Thai general election"), as well as [political demonstrations demanding democracy and monarchy reform](/wiki/2020%E2%80%932021_Thai_protests "2020–2021 Thai protests"). The current constitutional structure still allows *de facto* political influence by the [Royal Thai Armed Forces](/wiki/Royal_Thai_Armed_Forces "Royal Thai Armed Forces").[[12]](#cite_note-17) Thailand has also faced border tensions from the [2008–2011 Cambodian–Thai border crisis](/wiki/2008%E2%80%932011_Cambodian%E2%80%93Thai_border_crisis "2008–2011 Cambodian–Thai border crisis") extending to the [2025 Cambodia–Thailand border crisis](/wiki/2025_Cambodia%E2%80%93Thailand_border_crisis "2025 Cambodia–Thailand border crisis") involving territorial disputes and military clashes.

Currently, Thailand is recognised as a [developing country](/wiki/Developing_country "Developing country") and holds significant geopolitical importance in Southeast Asia. It is a [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") governed under a [parliamentary system](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system") with a [bicameral legislature](/wiki/National_Assembly_of_Thailand "National Assembly of Thailand") comprising the elected [House of Representatives](/wiki/House_of_Representatives_of_Thailand "House of Representatives of Thailand") and the appointed [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Thailand "Senate of Thailand"). Thailand is a member of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"), a major non-[NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") ally of the United States, and a founding member of the [Association of Southeast Asian Nations](/wiki/Association_of_Southeast_Asian_Nations "Association of Southeast Asian Nations") (ASEAN), as well as a [regional power](/wiki/Regional_power "Regional power"). The Royal Thai Armed Forces are among the largest military forces in Southeast Asia. Thailand's economy is the second-largest in the region and the 23rd globally by [purchasing power parity](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity"), ranking 29th by [gross domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product"). Thailand is classified as a [newly industrialised country](/wiki/Newly_industrialized_country "Newly industrialized country"), with manufacturing, agriculture, and [tourism](/wiki/Tourism_in_Thailand "Tourism in Thailand") being the main economic sectors.[[13]](#cite_note-18)[[14]](#cite_note-19)

## Etymology

Thailand[[iii]](#cite_note-endo-10) was known by outsiders before 1939 as *Siam*.[[v]](#cite_note-siam-12) According to [George Cœdès](/wiki/George_C%C5%93d%C3%A8s "George Cœdès"), the word *Thai* ([ไทย](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E0%B9%84%E0%B8%97%E0%B8%A2 "wikt:ไทย")) means 'free man' in the Thai language, "differentiating the Thai from the natives encompassed in Thai society as serfs".[[15]](#cite_note-Coedes_1968-20): 197 According to [Chit Phumisak](/wiki/Chit_Phumisak "Chit Phumisak"), Thai (ไท) simply means 'people' or 'human being'; his investigation shows that some rural areas used the word "Thai" instead of the usual Thai word *khon* ([คน](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E0%B8%84%E0%B8%99 "wikt:คน")) for people.[[16]](#cite_note-Phumisak_1992-21) According to [Michel Ferlus](/wiki/Michel_Ferlus "Michel Ferlus"), the ethnonyms Thai-Tai (or Thay-Tay) would have evolved from the etymon *\*k(ə)ri:* 'human being'.[[vi]](#cite_note-23)[[18]](#cite_note-24)

Thais often refer to their country using the polite form *prathet Thai* ([Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): ประเทศไทย). They also use the more colloquial term *mueang Thai* ([Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): เมืองไทย) or simply *Thai;* the word *[mueang](/wiki/Mueang "Mueang")*, archaically referring to a [city-state](/wiki/Mandala_(political_model) "Mandala (political model)"), is commonly used to refer to a city or town as the centre of a region. *Ratcha Anachak Thai* ([Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): ราชอาณาจักรไทย) means 'kingdom of Thailand' or 'kingdom of Thai'.

Etymologically, its components are: *ratcha* ([Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit_language "Sanskrit language"): राजन्, *[rājan](/wiki/R%C4%81jan "Rājan")*, 'king, royal, realm'), *ana-* ([Pali](/wiki/Pali "Pali") *āṇā* 'authority, command, power', itself from the Sanskrit आज्ञा, *ājñā*, of the same meaning), and *-chak* (from Sanskrit [चक्र](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E0%A4%9A%E0%A4%95%E0%A5%8D%E0%A4%B0 "wikt:चक्र") *cakra-* 'wheel', a symbol of power and rule).

The [Thai National Anthem](/wiki/Thai_National_Anthem "Thai National Anthem") ([Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): เพลงชาติ), written by [Luang Saranupraphan](/wiki/Luang_Saranupraphan "Luang Saranupraphan") during the patriotic 1930s, refers to the Thai nation as *prathet Thai* ([Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): ประเทศไทย). The first line of the national anthem is: *prathet thai ruam lueat nuea chat chuea thai* ([Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): ประเทศไทยรวมเลือดเนื้อชาติเชื้อไทย), 'Thailand is founded on blood and flesh'.[[19]](#cite_note-25)

The former name *Siam* may have originated from [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") श्याम (*śyāma*, 'dark')[[16]](#cite_note-Phumisak_1992-21) or [Mon](/wiki/Mon_language "Mon language") ရာမည (*rhmañña*, 'stranger'), probably the same root as *[Shan](/wiki/Shan_people "Shan people")* and *[Assam](/wiki/Assam "Assam")*.[[20]](#cite_note-26) The word *Śyâma* is possibly not the true origin, but a pre-designed deviation from its proper, original meaning.[[21]](#cite_note-27)[[22]](#cite_note-28) Another theory is the name derives from the Chinese calling this region 'Xian'.[[vii]](#cite_note-29)[[23]](#cite_note-Baker_2014-30): 8

The ancient Khmers used the word *Siam* to refer to people settled in the west [Chao Phraya River](/wiki/Chao_Phraya_River "Chao Phraya River") valley surrounding the ancient city of [Nakhon Pathom](/wiki/Nakhon_Pathom "Nakhon Pathom") in the present-day [central Thailand](/wiki/Central_Thailand "Central Thailand"); it may probably originate from the name of Lord [Krishna](/wiki/Krishna "Krishna"), which also called *Shyam*, as in the [Wat Sri Chum Inscription](/wiki/Wat_Sri_Chum_Inscription "Wat Sri Chum Inscription"), dated 13th century CE, mentions [Phra Maha Thera Sri Sattha](/w/index.php?title=Phra_Maha_Thera_Sri_Sattha&action=edit&redlink=1 "Phra Maha Thera Sri Sattha (page does not exist)") [[th](https://th.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E0%B8%9E%E0%B8%A3%E0%B8%B0%E0%B8%A1%E0%B8%AB%E0%B8%B2%E0%B9%80%E0%B8%96%E0%B8%A3%E0%B8%A8%E0%B8%A3%E0%B8%B5%E0%B8%A8%E0%B8%A3%E0%B8%B1%E0%B8%97%E0%B8%98%E0%B8%B2 "th:พระมหาเถรศรีศรัทธา")] came to restore [Phra Pathommachedi](/wiki/Phra_Pathommachedi "Phra Pathommachedi") at the city of Lord Krishna (Nakhon Pathom) in the early era of the [Sukhothai Kingdom](/wiki/Sukhothai_Kingdom "Sukhothai Kingdom").[[24]](#cite_note-sac-31)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Kingdom_%26_People_of_Siam_%28Vol_1%2C_1857%2C_15%29.svg/250px-Kingdom_%26_People_of_Siam_%28Vol_1%2C_1857%2C_15%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Kingdom_%26_People_of_Siam_(Vol_1,_1857,_15).svg)

*SPPM Mongkut Rex Siamensium*, [King Mongkut](/wiki/King_Mongkut "King Mongkut")'s signature

The signature of King [Mongkut](/wiki/Mongkut "Mongkut") (r. 1851–1868) reads *SPPM* (*Somdet Phra Poramenthra Maha*) *Mongkut Rex Siamensium* (Mongkut, King of the Siamese). This usage of the name in [the country's first international treaty](/wiki/Bowring_Treaty "Bowring Treaty") gave the name *Siam* official status, until 24 June 1939 when it was changed to *Thailand*.[[25]](#cite_note-32)

### Prehistory and origins

Main article: [Prehistoric Thailand](/wiki/Prehistoric_Thailand "Prehistoric Thailand")

See also: [History of Thailand](/wiki/History_of_Thailand "History of Thailand") and [Tai peoples](/wiki/Tai_peoples "Tai peoples")

There is evidence of continuous human habitation in present-day Thailand from 20,000 years ago to the present day.[[26]](#cite_note-LePoer_1989-33): 4 The earliest evidence of rice growing is dated at 2,000 BCE.[[27]](#cite_note-Baker_2017-34): 4 Areas comprising what is now Thailand participated in the Maritime Jade Road, as ascertained by archeological research. The trading network existed for 3,000 years, between 2000 BCE to 1000 CE.[[28]](#cite_note-35)[[29]](#cite_note-36)[[30]](#cite_note-37)[[31]](#cite_note-38) Bronze appeared c. 1,250–1,000 BCE.[[27]](#cite_note-Baker_2017-34): 4 The site of [Ban Chiang](/wiki/Ban_Chiang "Ban Chiang") in northeast Thailand currently ranks as the earliest known centre of copper and bronze production in Southeast Asia.[[32]](#cite_note-39) Iron appeared around 500 BCE.[[27]](#cite_note-Baker_2017-34): 5

The [Kingdom of Funan](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Funan "Kingdom of Funan") was the first and most powerful Southeast Asian kingdom at the time (2nd century BCE).[[26]](#cite_note-LePoer_1989-33): 5 The [Mon people](/wiki/Mon_people "Mon people") established the principalities of [Dvaravati](/wiki/Dvaravati "Dvaravati") and Kingdom of [Hariphunchai](/wiki/Hariphunchai "Hariphunchai") in the 6th century. The [Khmer people](/wiki/Khmer_people "Khmer people") established the [Khmer empire](/wiki/Khmer_empire "Khmer empire"), centred in [Angkor](/wiki/Angkor "Angkor"), in the 9th century.[[26]](#cite_note-LePoer_1989-33): 7 [Tambralinga](/wiki/Tambralinga "Tambralinga"), a Malay state controlling trade through the [Malacca Strait](/wiki/Strait_of_Malacca "Strait of Malacca"), rose in the 10th century.[[26]](#cite_note-LePoer_1989-33): 5 The Indochina peninsula was heavily influenced by the [culture and religions of India](/wiki/Indianized_kingdom "Indianized kingdom") from the time of the Kingdom of Funan to that of the Khmer Empire.[[33]](#cite_note-40)

The [Thai people](/wiki/Thai_people "Thai people") are of the [Tai ethnic group](/wiki/Tai_peoples "Tai peoples"), characterised by common linguistic roots.[[34]](#cite_note-Wyatt_1984-41): 2 Chinese chronicles first mention the Tai peoples in the 6th century BCE. While there are many assumptions regarding the origin of Tai peoples, [David K. Wyatt](/wiki/David_K._Wyatt "David K. Wyatt"), a historian of Thailand, argued that their ancestors who at present inhabit Laos, Thailand, Myanmar, India, and China came from the [Điện Biên Phủ](/wiki/%C4%90i%E1%BB%87n_Bi%C3%AAn_Ph%E1%BB%A7 "Điện Biên Phủ") area between the 5th and the 8th century.[[34]](#cite_note-Wyatt_1984-41): 6

Thai people began migrating into present-day Thailand gradually from the 6th to 11th century, which Mon and Khmer people occupied at the time.[[35]](#cite_note-42) Thus Thai culture was influenced by Indian, Mon, and Khmer cultures.[[36]](#cite_note-43): 203 Tai people intermixed with various ethnic and cultural groups in the region, resulting in many groups of present-day Thai people.[[viii]](#cite_note-44) Genetic evidences suggested that ethnolinguistics could not accurately predict the origins of the Thais.[[37]](#cite_note-genetic-45)[[38]](#cite_note-genetic2-46)[[39]](#cite_note-genetic3-47) [Sujit Wongthes](/wiki/Sujit_Wongthes "Sujit Wongthes") argued that Thai is not a race or ethnicity but a culture group.[[40]](#cite_note-48)

According to French historian [George Cœdès](/wiki/George_C%C5%93d%C3%A8s "George Cœdès"), "The Thai first enter history of [Farther India](/wiki/Farther_India "Farther India") in the eleventh century with the mention of *Syam* slaves or prisoners of war in [Champa](/wiki/Champa "Champa") epigraphy", and "in the twelfth century, the [bas-reliefs](/wiki/Bas-relief "Bas-relief") of [Angkor Wat](/wiki/Angkor_Wat "Angkor Wat")" where "a group of warriors" are described as *Syam*,[[15]](#cite_note-Coedes_1968-20): 190–191, 194–195 though Cham accounts do not indicate the origins of *Syam* or what ethnic group they belonged to.[[41]](#cite_note-Smith_2019-49) The origins and ethnicity of the *Syam* remain unclear, with some literature suggesting that *Syam* refers to the [Shan people](/wiki/Shan_people "Shan people"), the [Bru people](/wiki/Bru_people "Bru people"), or the [Brau people](/wiki/Brau_people "Brau people").[[41]](#cite_note-Smith_2019-49)[[42]](#cite_note-50) However, mainland Southeast Asian sources from before the fourteenth century primarily used the word *Syam* as an [ethnonym](/wiki/Ethnonym "Ethnonym"), referring to those who belonged to a separate cultural category different from the Khmer, Cham, Bagan, or Mon. This contrasts with the Chinese sources, where *Xian* was used as a [toponym](/wiki/Toponym "Toponym").[[41]](#cite_note-Smith_2019-49)

### Early Tai confederate cities (691 BCE – 13th century CE)

See also: [Initial states of Thailand](/wiki/Initial_states_of_Thailand "Initial states of Thailand") and [Lan Na](/wiki/Lan_Na "Lan Na")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Tai_Migration.svg/250px-Tai_Migration.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Tai_Migration.svg)

Map showing geographic distribution of [Kra–Dai linguistic family](/wiki/Kra%E2%80%93Dai_languages "Kra–Dai languages"). Arrows represent general pattern of the migration of [Tai-speaking tribes](/wiki/Tai_peoples "Tai peoples") along the rivers and over the lower passes.[[27]](#cite_note-Baker_2017-34): 27

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Location_Lanna_%28under_King_Tilok%29.png/250px-Location_Lanna_%28under_King_Tilok%29.png)](/wiki/File:Location_Lanna_(under_King_Tilok).png)

[Lan Na](/wiki/Lan_Na "Lan Na") kingdom during the reign of [Tilokaraj](/wiki/Tilokaraj "Tilokaraj") (r. 1441–1487)

Theoretically, [Tai-Kadai](/wiki/Tai-Kadai "Tai-Kadai")-speaking people formed as early as the 12th century BCE in the middle of the [Yangtze basin](/wiki/Yangtze_basin "Yangtze basin"). Some groups later migrated south to [Guangxi](/wiki/Guangxi "Guangxi").[[43]](#cite_note-Chamberlain_2016-51) However, after several bloody centuries against [Chinese](/wiki/China "China") influence in [Guangxi](/wiki/Guangxi "Guangxi") between 333 BCE and the 11th century, hundreds of thousands of Tais were killed,[[44]](#cite_note-52): 5[[45]](#cite_note-53): 193, 239–249 thus, [Tai people](/wiki/Tai_people "Tai people") began to move southwestward along the rivers and over the lower passes into the mountain north of [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") and river valleys in present-day [Assam](/wiki/Assam "Assam") of [India](/wiki/India "India").[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBakerPhongpaichit201726-54)[[47]](#cite_note-55) Some evidence indicates that the ancestors of Tai people migrated en masse southwestwards out of Yunnan only after the 1253 [Mongol invasion of Dali](/wiki/Uriyangkhadai#Invasion_of_Yunnan_and_Tibet "Uriyangkhadai"); however, it is not generally accepted.[[48]](#cite_note-56): 38

Tais defeated [indigenous tribes](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples "Indigenous peoples") and emerged as the new power in the new region. As a result, several Tai city-states were established, scattered from [Điện Biên Phủ](/wiki/%C4%90i%E1%BB%87n_Bi%C3%AAn_Ph%E1%BB%A7 "Điện Biên Phủ") in present-day northwestern [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam") and highland [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") to [northeastern India](/wiki/Northeastern_India "Northeastern India").[[49]](#cite_note-jumsai1967-57)[[50]](#cite_note-iml-58)[[51]](#cite_note-PittayawatPittayaporn-59)

According to the *Simhanavati legend* given in several chronicles, the first Tai city-state in northern Thailand, [Singhanavati](/wiki/Singhanavati "Singhanavati"), was founded around the 7th century.[[52]](#cite_note-wieng-60): 5, 9 However, several modern [geology](/wiki/Geology "Geology") and [archaeology](/wiki/Archaeology "Archaeology") studies found that its centre, Yonok Nahaphan, dates from 691 BCE–545 CE;[[52]](#cite_note-wieng-60): 7[[53]](#cite_note-fine-61)[[54]](#cite_note-era-62)[[55]](#cite_note-mgryo-63) this roughly coincides with the establishment of [Shan States](/wiki/Shan_people#History "Shan people"), another Tai's federated principalities in the present-day northeast [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar").[[56]](#cite_note-64)[[57]](#cite_note-65)[[58]](#cite_note-66)[[59]](#cite_note-67) as well as [Muang Sua](/wiki/Muang_Sua "Muang Sua") ([Luang Prabang](/wiki/Luang_Prabang "Luang Prabang")) in the east.[[60]](#cite_note-Viravond-68)[[61]](#cite_note-Manich-69)

After Singhanavati was submerged below [Chiang Saen Lake](/wiki/Chiang_Saen_Lake "Chiang Saen Lake") due to an [earthquake](/wiki/Earthquake "Earthquake") in 545,[[53]](#cite_note-fine-61)[[55]](#cite_note-mgryo-63)[[62]](#cite_note-70) the survivors then founded a new seat at [Wieng–Prueksha](/w/index.php?title=Wieng%E2%80%93Prueksha&action=edit&redlink=1 "Wieng–Prueksha (page does not exist)") [[th](https://th.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E0%B9%80%E0%B8%A7%E0%B8%B5%E0%B8%A2%E0%B8%87%E0%B8%9B%E0%B8%A3%E0%B8%B6%E0%B8%81%E0%B8%A9%E0%B8%B2 "th:เวียงปรึกษา")]. The kingdom lasted for another 93 years.[[63]](#cite_note-71)

In addition to [Singhanavati](/wiki/Singhanavati "Singhanavati"), another northern principality probably related to the Tai people, [Ngoenyang](/wiki/Ngoenyang "Ngoenyang"), was established as the successor of Singhanavati in 638 by [Lavachakkaraj](/w/index.php?title=Lavachakkaraj&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lavachakkaraj (page does not exist)") [[th](https://th.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E0%B8%9B%E0%B8%B9%E0%B9%88%E0%B9%80%E0%B8%88%E0%B9%89%E0%B8%B2%E0%B8%A5%E0%B8%B2%E0%B8%A7%E0%B8%88%E0%B8%81 "th:ปู่เจ้าลาวจก")], also centred in [Wieng–Prueksha](/w/index.php?title=Wieng%E2%80%93Prueksha&action=edit&redlink=1 "Wieng–Prueksha (page does not exist)") [[th](https://th.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E0%B9%80%E0%B8%A7%E0%B8%B5%E0%B8%A2%E0%B8%87%E0%B8%9B%E0%B8%A3%E0%B8%B6%E0%B8%81%E0%B8%A9%E0%B8%B2 "th:เวียงปรึกษา")] (present-day [Mae Sai District](/wiki/Mae_Sai_District "Mae Sai District"), [Chiang Rai](/wiki/Chiang_Rai_province "Chiang Rai province")).[[23]](#cite_note-Baker_2014-30): 8 Its seat was moved to [Chiang Mai](/wiki/Chiang_Mai "Chiang Mai") in 1262 by King [Mangrai](/wiki/Mangrai "Mangrai"), which considered the foundation of the [Lan Na](/wiki/Lan_Na "Lan Na") kingdom. Mangrai unified the surrounding area and also created a network of states through political alliances to the east and north of the [Mekong](/wiki/Mekong "Mekong"). His dynasty would rule the kingdom continuously for the next two centuries.[[23]](#cite_note-Baker_2014-30): 8 [Lan Na](/wiki/Lan_Na "Lan Na") expanded its territory southward and annexed the [Mon](/wiki/Mon_people "Mon people") [Hariphunchai](/wiki/Hariphunchai "Hariphunchai") of [Dvaravati](/wiki/Dvaravati "Dvaravati") in 1292.[[64]](#cite_note-Coedes-72): 208

In the late tenth century, Tai people began to migrate further south to the present-day upper [central Thailand](/wiki/Central_Thailand "Central Thailand").[[65]](#cite_note-ch-73): 46–9, 83–6 Around the 1100s period, several cities in this area, such as Songkwae, Sawankhalok, and Chakangrao, were ruled by the Tai people, eventually launching several battles against the pre-existing [Mon](/wiki/Mon_people "Mon people") of [Lavo](/wiki/Lavo_Kingdom "Lavo Kingdom"), who had been falling under [Chenla](/wiki/Chenla "Chenla") and [Khmer](/wiki/Khmer_Empire "Khmer Empire") influences since the 7th century, thus bringing the establishment of the Tai people's independent state, [Sukhothai Kingdom](/wiki/Sukhothai_Kingdom "Sukhothai Kingdom"), in the upper [Chao Phraya River](/wiki/Chao_Phraya_River "Chao Phraya River") valley in 1238.[[34]](#cite_note-Wyatt_1984-41): 52–3

The earliest conflict between [Tai people](/wiki/Tai_people "Tai people") and the preexisting ethnics was recorded in the mid-4th century when the ruler of [Singhanavati](/wiki/Singhanavati "Singhanavati"), [Pangkharat](/w/index.php?title=Pangkharat&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pangkharat (page does not exist)") [[th](https://th.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E0%B8%9E%E0%B8%A3%E0%B8%B0%E0%B9%80%E0%B8%88%E0%B9%89%E0%B8%B2%E0%B8%9E%E0%B8%B1%E0%B8%87%E0%B8%84%E0%B8%A3%E0%B8%B2%E0%B8%8A "th:พระเจ้าพังคราช")], forcibly lost the seat at Yonok to King Khom from Umongasela (present-day [Fang](/wiki/Fang_district "Fang district")). He then fled to Vieng Si Tuang (เวียงศรีทวง; present-day Wiang Phang Kham, [Mae Sai district](/wiki/Mae_Sai_district "Mae Sai district")) but had to send tributes to Yonok annually until his son, [Phrom](/wiki/Phrom "Phrom"), took back Yonok and expelled King Khom from Umongasela.[[66]](#cite_note-krai-74)[[67]](#cite_note-prom-75)[[68]](#cite_note-chiangrai-76) [Phrom](/wiki/Phrom "Phrom") also marched the troops south to occupy [Chakangrao](/wiki/Kamphaeng_Phet_province "Kamphaeng Phet province") from the enemy as well as founding the city of [Songkwae](/wiki/Phitsanulok "Phitsanulok").[[66]](#cite_note-krai-74) Some historians suggest that [Lavo](/wiki/Lavo_Kingdom "Lavo Kingdom")'s capital, [Lopburi](/wiki/Lopburi "Lopburi"), was once seized by [Phrom](/wiki/Phrom "Phrom").[[66]](#cite_note-krai-74) In contrast, [Tai people](/wiki/Tai_people "Tai people") instead established relationships with Mon via [royal intermarriages](/wiki/Royal_intermarriage "Royal intermarriage").[[65]](#cite_note-ch-73): 46–9, 83–6

### Mon and Lavo Kingdoms (5th century CE – 1388 CE)

See also: [Mon people](/wiki/Mon_people "Mon people"), [Thai people](/wiki/Thai_people "Thai people"), [Mon kingdoms](/wiki/Mon_kingdoms "Mon kingdoms"), and [Lavo Kingdom](/wiki/Lavo_Kingdom "Lavo Kingdom")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Mon_political_entities.png/250px-Mon_political_entities.png)](/wiki/File:Mon_political_entities.png)

Political entities of the Mon people around the 6th–7th centuries

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/DvaravatiMapThailand.png/250px-DvaravatiMapThailand.png)](/wiki/File:DvaravatiMapThailand.png)

Map of settlements of Dvaravati culture from the 6th to 9th centuries

The [Chao Phraya River](/wiki/Chao_Phraya_River "Chao Phraya River") in what is now [central Thailand](/wiki/Central_Thailand "Central Thailand") had once been the home of the [Mon](/wiki/Mon_people "Mon people") [Dvaravati](/wiki/Dvaravati "Dvaravati") culture, which prevailed from the seventh century to the tenth century.[[69]](#cite_note-77) Samuel Beal discovered the polity among the Chinese writings on Southeast Asia as "Duoluobodi". During the early 20th century, archaeological excavations led by [George Coedès](/wiki/George_Coed%C3%A8s "George Coedès") found [Nakhon Pathom Province](/wiki/Nakhon_Pathom_Province "Nakhon Pathom Province") to be a centre of Dvaravati culture. The two most important sites were Nakorn Pathom and U Thong (in modern [Suphan Buri Province](/wiki/Suphan_Buri_Province "Suphan Buri Province")). The inscriptions of Dvaravati were in [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") and [Mon](/wiki/Mon_language "Mon language") using the script derived from the [Pallava alphabet](/wiki/Pallava_alphabet "Pallava alphabet") of the [South Indian](/wiki/South_India "South India") Pallava dynasty.

It is believed that the Dvaravati borrowed [Theravada Buddhism](/wiki/Theravada "Theravada") through its contacts with Sri Lanka, while the ruling class participated in [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") rites. [Dvaravati art](/wiki/Dvaravati_art "Dvaravati art"), including the Buddha sculptures and [stupas](/wiki/Stupa "Stupa"), showed strong similarities to those of the [Gupta Empire](/wiki/Gupta_Empire "Gupta Empire") of India. The eastern parts of the Chao Phraya valley were subjected to a more Khmer and Hindu influence as the inscriptions are found in Khmer and Sanskrit.[[70]](#cite_note-78)

Dvaravati was a network of city-states paying tribute to more powerful ones according to the [mandala political model](/wiki/Mandala_(Southeast_Asian_political_model) "Mandala (Southeast Asian political model)"). Dvaravati culture expanded into [Isan](/wiki/Isan "Isan") as well as south as far as the [Kra Isthmus](/wiki/Kra_Isthmus "Kra Isthmus"). Dvaravati culture lost its influence around the tenth century when they submitted to the more unified Lavo-Khmer polity.

Around the tenth century, the city-states of Dvaravati merged into the mandalas of: [Lavo](/wiki/Lavo "Lavo") (modern [Lopburi](/wiki/Lopburi "Lopburi")) and [Suvarnabhumi](/wiki/Suvarnabhumi "Suvarnabhumi") (modern [Suphan Buri](/wiki/Suphan_Buri "Suphan Buri")). According to a legend in the Northern Chronicles, in 903, a king of [Tambralinga](/wiki/Tambralinga "Tambralinga") invaded and took Lavo and installed a Malay prince on the Lavo throne. The Malay prince was married to a Khmer princess who had fled an Angkorian dynastic bloodbath. The son of the couple contested the Khmer throne and became [Suryavarman I](/wiki/Suryavarman_I "Suryavarman I") (1006–1050), thus bringing Lavo under Khmer domination through marital union. Suryavarman I also expanded into the [Khorat Plateau](/wiki/Khorat_Plateau "Khorat Plateau") (later styled "Isan"), constructing many temples.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/%E0%B8%9B%E0%B8%A3%E0%B8%B2%E0%B8%AA%E0%B8%B2%E0%B8%97%E0%B8%9B%E0%B8%A3%E0%B8%B2%E0%B8%87%E0%B8%84%E0%B9%8C%E0%B8%AA%E0%B8%B2%E0%B8%A1%E0%B8%A2%E0%B8%AD%E0%B8%942.jpg/250px-%E0%B8%9B%E0%B8%A3%E0%B8%B2%E0%B8%AA%E0%B8%B2%E0%B8%97%E0%B8%9B%E0%B8%A3%E0%B8%B2%E0%B8%87%E0%B8%84%E0%B9%8C%E0%B8%AA%E0%B8%B2%E0%B8%A1%E0%B8%A2%E0%B8%AD%E0%B8%942.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E0%B8%9B%E0%B8%A3%E0%B8%B2%E0%B8%AA%E0%B8%B2%E0%B8%97%E0%B8%9B%E0%B8%A3%E0%B8%B2%E0%B8%87%E0%B8%84%E0%B9%8C%E0%B8%AA%E0%B8%B2%E0%B8%A1%E0%B8%A2%E0%B8%AD%E0%B8%942.jpg)

Wat [Phra Prang Sam Yod](/wiki/Phra_Prang_Sam_Yod "Phra Prang Sam Yod"), a 13th-century Angkorian temple in [Lopburi](/wiki/Lopburi "Lopburi").

After the [Angkor](/wiki/Angkor "Angkor") lost Lavo to [Suphannabhum](/wiki/Suphannabhum "Suphannabhum") princes in 1052, the younger prince [Chandrachota](/wiki/Chandrachota "Chandrachota") claimed the throne. His son, [Narai I](/wiki/Narai_I "Narai I"), moved Lavo's seat to [Ayodhya](/wiki/Phra_Nakhon_Si_Ayutthaya_(city) "Phra Nakhon Si Ayutthaya (city)") in the 1080s. This resulted in the throne of Lavo's Lavaburi being vacant from 1087 to 1106 (or ruled by unknown kings), [Si Satchanalai](/wiki/Si_Satchanalai "Si Satchanalai") king, [Kesariraja](/wiki/Kesariraja "Kesariraja") who is of [Mon](/wiki/Mon_people "Mon people")'s [Chaliang](/wiki/Mueang_Chaliang "Mueang Chaliang") and [Tai](/wiki/Tai_people "Tai people")'s [Chiang Saen](/wiki/Chiang_Saen "Chiang Saen") lineages, took over the seat. An attempt to re-expand influence to Lavo by the Angkor occurred in 1181 when [Jayavarman VII](/wiki/Jayavarman_VII "Jayavarman VII") appointed his lineage Narupatidnavarman to govern Lavapura.[[71]](#cite_note-indra-79) Due to this political pressure, a Tai ruler Sri Thammasokkarat
(ศรีธรรมโศกราช), who was also from Si Satchanalai, fled to [Nakhon Si Thammarat](/wiki/Nakhon_Si_Thammarat_Kingdom "Nakhon Si Thammarat Kingdom").[[72]](#cite_note-ori-80): 38–39 Sri Thammasokkarat married to princess of Dhanyapura (Dong Mae Nang Mueang in the present-day [Nakhon Sawan province](/wiki/Nakhon_Sawan_province "Nakhon Sawan province")), and their descendants ruled [Tambralinga](/wiki/Tambralinga "Tambralinga") until the late 13th century.[[73]](#cite_note-pad-81): 290–2 [The Customs of Cambodia](/wiki/The_Customs_of_Cambodia "The Customs of Cambodia") of [Zhou Daguan](/wiki/Zhou_Daguan "Zhou Daguan"), as an official delegation sent by the [Yuan dynasty](/wiki/Yuan_dynasty "Yuan dynasty") to [Angkor](/wiki/Angkor "Angkor") from 1296 to 1297, says the Siamese people exerted significant influence over Lavo's [Lavapura](/wiki/Lopburi "Lopburi") and appeared in huge numbers in the Angkorian capital of [Yaśodharapura](/wiki/Ya%C5%9Bodharapura "Yaśodharapura").[[74]](#cite_note-Reviewed_Work:_A_record_of_Cambodia-82)[[75]](#cite_note-siamese-83)

[Prang Sam Yot](/wiki/Phra_Prang_Sam_Yot "Phra Prang Sam Yot") was built during this period.[[76]](#cite_note-sitham-84): 170 However, Lavapura was probably taken back by a Tai royal from Phraek Si Racha (แพรกศรีราชา; present-day [Sankhaburi](/wiki/Sankhaburi_district "Sankhaburi district")) in the 13th century.[[65]](#cite_note-ch-73): 109 During this era, Angkor's power waned due to the weak rule and feuding began in the Angkor.[[77]](#cite_note-Higham1-85): 120

All of the turmoil, as mentioned earlier, also led to the independence declaration of the [Sukhothai Kingdom](/wiki/Sukhothai_Kingdom "Sukhothai Kingdom") in 1238.[[78]](#cite_note-lost-86)

#### The earliest evidence mentioning the Siamese

The earliest evidence to mention the Siam people are stone inscriptions found in [Angkor Borei](/wiki/Angkor_Borei "Angkor Borei") of [Funan](/wiki/Funan "Funan") (K.557 and K.600), dated 661 CE, the slave's name is mentioned as "Ku Sayam" meaning "Sayam female slaves" (Ku is a prefix used to refer to female slaves in the pre-Angkorian era), and the Takéo inscriptions (K.79) written in 682 during the reign of [Bhavavarman II](/wiki/Bhavavarman_II "Bhavavarman II") of [Chenla](/wiki/Chenla "Chenla") also mention Siam Nobel: *Sāraṇnoya Poña Sayam*, which was transcribed into English as: *the rice field that was given to the poña (noble rank) who was called Sayam (Siam)*.[[79]](#cite_note-eth-87)

The [Song Huiyao Jigao](/wiki/Song_Huiyao_Jigao "Song Huiyao Jigao") (960–1279) indicate Siamese people settled in the west [central Thailand](/wiki/Central_Thailand "Central Thailand") and their state was called *Xiān guó* ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 暹國), while the eastern plain belonged to the Mon of [Lavo](/wiki/Lavo_Kingdom "Lavo Kingdom") ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 羅渦國),[[80]](#cite_note-ngr-88) who later fell under the [Chenla](/wiki/Chenla "Chenla") and [Khmer](/wiki/Khmer_Empire "Khmer Empire") hegemony around the 7th–9th centuries.[[81]](#cite_note-89) Those Mon political entities, which also included [Haripuñjaya](/wiki/Haripu%C3%B1jaya "Haripuñjaya") in the north and several city-states in the [northeast](/wiki/Northeastern_Thailand "Northeastern Thailand"), are collectively called [Dvaravati](/wiki/Dvaravati "Dvaravati").

However, the states of Siamese and Lavo were later merged via the [royal intermarriage](/wiki/Royal_intermarriage "Royal intermarriage") and became [Ayutthaya Kingdom](/wiki/Ayutthaya_Kingdom "Ayutthaya Kingdom") in the mid-14th century,[[80]](#cite_note-ngr-88) while the southwestern [Isan](/wiki/Isan "Isan") principalities, centred in [Phanom Rung](/wiki/Phanom_Rung_Historical_Park "Phanom Rung Historical Park") and [Phimai](/wiki/Phimai "Phimai"), later pledged allegiance to Siamese's Ayutthaya during the reign of [Borommarachathirat II](/wiki/Borommarachathirat_II "Borommarachathirat II") (r. 1424–1448).[[82]](#cite_note-90) The remaining principal city-states in [Isan](/wiki/Isan "Isan") region became [Lan Xang](/wiki/Lan_Xang "Lan Xang") around 1353 after the twin cities of [Muang Sua](/wiki/Muang_Sua "Muang Sua") ([Luang Prabang](/wiki/Luang_Prabang "Luang Prabang")) and *Vieng Chan Vieng Kham* ([Vientiane](/wiki/Vientiane "Vientiane")) became independent following the death of the [Sukhothai](/wiki/Sukhothai_Kingdom "Sukhothai Kingdom") king [Ram Khamhaeng](/wiki/Ram_Khamhaeng "Ram Khamhaeng").[[83]](#cite_note-91): 51

According to the Wat Kud Tae inscription (K.1105), dated c. 7th century, during the period that the eastern Mon entity, [Lavo](/wiki/Lavo_Kingdom "Lavo Kingdom"), was strongly influenced by the [Chenla](/wiki/Chenla "Chenla"), the Siamese in the west also established a [royal intermarriage](/wiki/Royal_intermarriage "Royal intermarriage") with [Chenla](/wiki/Chenla "Chenla") as Sri Chakatham, prince of Sambhuka (ศามภูกะ, in the present-day [Ratchaburi province](/wiki/Ratchaburi_province "Ratchaburi province")), married to a princess of [Isanavarman I](/wiki/Isanavarman_I "Isanavarman I"), and two [mandalas](/wiki/Mandala_(political_model) "Mandala (political model)") then became an ally.[[84]](#cite_note-ally-92) After [Chenla](/wiki/Chenla "Chenla") sieged [Funan](/wiki/Funan "Funan") and moved the centre to [Angkor](/wiki/Angkor "Angkor"), both Siamese and the Angkorian eventually marched the troops to attack [Vijaya](/wiki/Vijaya_(Champa) "Vijaya (Champa)") of [Champa](/wiki/Champa "Champa") in 1201 during the reign of [Jayavarman VII](/wiki/Jayavarman_VII "Jayavarman VII"), as recorded in the Cho-Dinh inscription (C.3).[[85]](#cite_note-c3-93)

### Sukhothai Kingdom (1238–1438)

Main article: [Sukhothai Kingdom](/wiki/Sukhothai_Kingdom "Sukhothai Kingdom")

[Sukhothai Kingdom](/wiki/Sukhothai_Kingdom "Sukhothai Kingdom")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Southeast_Asian_history_-_13th_century.png/120px-Southeast_Asian_history_-_13th_century.png)](/wiki/File:Southeast_Asian_history_-_13th_century.png)

Sukhothai and neighbours, end of 13th century CE

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Phra_Achana_%28I%29.jpg/250px-Phra_Achana_%28I%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Phra_Achana_(I).jpg)

Phra Achana, Wat Si Chum, Sukhothai Historical Park

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Sukhothai_Historical_Park.jpg/250px-Sukhothai_Historical_Park.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sukhothai_Historical_Park.jpg)

The ruins of Wat Mahathat, [Sukhothai Historical Park](/wiki/Sukhothai_Historical_Park "Sukhothai Historical Park")

After the decline of the Khmer Empire and [Kingdom of Pagan](/wiki/Pagan_Kingdom "Pagan Kingdom") in the early 13th century, various states thrived in their place. The domains of Tai people existed from the northeast of present-day India to the north of present-day Laos and to the [Malay Peninsula](/wiki/Malay_Peninsula "Malay Peninsula").[[34]](#cite_note-Wyatt_1984-41): 38–9 During the 13th century, Tai people had already settled in the core land of [Dvaravati](/wiki/Dvaravati "Dvaravati") and [Lavo Kingdom](/wiki/Lavo_Kingdom "Lavo Kingdom") to [Nakhon Si Thammarat](/wiki/Nakhon_Si_Thammarat_Kingdom "Nakhon Si Thammarat Kingdom") in the south. There are, however, no records detailing the arrival of the Tais.[[34]](#cite_note-Wyatt_1984-41): 50–1

Around 1240, [Pho Khun Bang Klang Hao](/wiki/Si_Inthrathit "Si Inthrathit"), a local Tai ruler, rallied the people to rebel against the Khmer. He later crowned himself the first king of [Sukhothai Kingdom](/wiki/Sukhothai_Kingdom "Sukhothai Kingdom") in 1238.[[34]](#cite_note-Wyatt_1984-41): 52–3 Mainstream Thai historians count Sukhothai as the first kingdom of Thai people. Sukhothai expanded furthest during the reign of [Ram Khamhaeng](/wiki/Ram_Khamhaeng "Ram Khamhaeng") (r. 1279–1298). However, it was mostly a network of local lords who swore fealty to Sukhothai, not directly controlled by it.[[34]](#cite_note-Wyatt_1984-41): 55–56 He is believed have invented [Thai script](/wiki/Thai_script "Thai script") and Thai ceramics were an important export in his era. Sukhothai embraced [Theravada](/wiki/Theravada "Theravada") [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") in the reign of [Maha Thammaracha I](/wiki/Maha_Thammaracha_I "Maha Thammaracha I") (1347–1368).

### Ayutthaya Kingdom (1351–1767)

Main articles: [Ayutthaya Kingdom](/wiki/Ayutthaya_Kingdom "Ayutthaya Kingdom"), [Galeote Pereira](/wiki/Galeote_Pereira "Galeote Pereira"), [Burmese–Siamese War (1547–1549)](/wiki/Burmese%E2%80%93Siamese_War_(1547%E2%80%931549) "Burmese–Siamese War (1547–1549)"), and [Burmese–Siamese War (1584–1593)](/wiki/Burmese%E2%80%93Siamese_War_(1584%E2%80%931593) "Burmese–Siamese War (1584–1593)")

[Ayutthaya Kingdom](/wiki/Ayutthaya_Kingdom "Ayutthaya Kingdom")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Map_of_Ayutthaya_Kingdom_in_1390.jpg/120px-Map_of_Ayutthaya_Kingdom_in_1390.jpg)](/wiki/File:Map_of_Ayutthaya_Kingdom_in_1390.jpg)

Ayutthaya and neighbors, c. 1390 CE

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Map_of_Ayutthaya_Kingdom_in_1545.jpg/120px-Map_of_Ayutthaya_Kingdom_in_1545.jpg)](/wiki/File:Map_of_Ayutthaya_Kingdom_in_1545.jpg)

Ayutthaya and neighbors, c. 1540 CE

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Bird%27s_Eye_View_of_Iudiad_City_%28Ayutthaya%29_c1665.jpg/250px-Bird%27s_Eye_View_of_Iudiad_City_%28Ayutthaya%29_c1665.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bird%27s_Eye_View_of_Iudiad_City_(Ayutthaya)_c1665.jpg)

Painting of Ayutthaya city c. 1665, by [Johannes Vingboons](/wiki/Johannes_Vingboons "Johannes Vingboons")

According to the most widely accepted version of its origin, the Ayutthaya Kingdom rose from the earlier, nearby [Lavo Kingdom](/wiki/Lavo_Kingdom "Lavo Kingdom") and Suvarnabhumi with [Uthong](/wiki/Uthong "Uthong") as its first king. Ayutthaya was a patchwork of self-governing principalities and tributary provinces owing allegiance to the King of Ayutthaya under the [mandala system](/wiki/Mandala_(political_model) "Mandala (political model)").[[86]](#cite_note-94): 355 Its initial expansion was through conquest and political marriage.

Before the end of the 15th century, Ayutthaya invaded the Khmer Empire three times and sacked its capital [Angkor](/wiki/Angkor "Angkor").[[87]](#cite_note-95): 26 Ayutthaya then became a regional power in place of the Khmer. Constant interference of Sukhothai effectively made it a vassal state of Ayutthaya and it was finally incorporated into the kingdom. [Borommatrailokkanat](/wiki/Borommatrailokkanat "Borommatrailokkanat") brought about bureaucratic reforms which lasted into the 20th century and created a system of social hierarchy called *[sakdina](/wiki/Sakdina "Sakdina")*, where male commoners were conscripted as [corvée](/wiki/Corv%C3%A9e "Corvée") labourers for six months a year.[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 107 Ayutthaya was interested in the [Malay Peninsula](/wiki/Malay_Peninsula "Malay Peninsula") but failed to conquer the [Malacca Sultanate](/wiki/Malacca_Sultanate "Malacca Sultanate"), which was supported by the Chinese [Ming dynasty](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty").[[26]](#cite_note-LePoer_1989-33): 11, 13

European contact and trade started in the early-16th century, with the [envoy](/wiki/Duarte_Fernandes "Duarte Fernandes") of [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_people "Portuguese people") duke [Afonso de Albuquerque](/wiki/Afonso_de_Albuquerque "Afonso de Albuquerque") in 1511. Portugal became an ally and ceded some soldiers to King Rama Thibodi II.[[89]](#cite_note-97) The Portuguese were followed in the 17th century by the French, Dutch, and English. Rivalry for supremacy over Chiang Mai and the Mon people pitted Ayutthaya against the Burmese Kingdom. Several wars with its ruling [Taungoo dynasty](/wiki/Taungoo_dynasty "Taungoo dynasty") starting in the 1540s in the reign of [Tabinshwehti](/wiki/Tabinshwehti "Tabinshwehti") and [Bayinnaung](/wiki/Bayinnaung "Bayinnaung") were ultimately ended with the [capture of the capital in 1570](/wiki/Burmese%E2%80%93Siamese_War_(1568%E2%80%9370) "Burmese–Siamese War (1568–70)").[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 146–7 Then a brief period of vassalage to Burma until [Naresuan](/wiki/Naresuan "Naresuan") proclaimed independence in 1584 followed.[[23]](#cite_note-Baker_2014-30): 11

Ayutthaya sought to improve relations with European powers for many successive reigns. The kingdom especially prospered during cosmopolitan [Narai](/wiki/Narai "Narai")'s reign (1656–1688), when some European travellers regarded Ayutthaya as an Asian great power, alongside China and India.[[27]](#cite_note-Baker_2017-34): ix However, growing French influence later in his reign was met with nationalist sentiment and eventually led to the [Siamese revolution of 1688](/wiki/Siamese_revolution_of_1688 "Siamese revolution of 1688").[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 185–6 Despite the revolution, overall relations remained stable, with French missionaries still actively preaching Christianity.[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 186

After a bloody period of dynastic struggle, Ayutthaya entered what has been called the Siamese "[golden age](/wiki/Golden_age "Golden age")", a relatively peaceful episode in the second quarter of the 18th century where [art](/wiki/Thai_art "Thai art"), [literature](/wiki/Thai_literature "Thai literature"), and learning flourished. There were seldom foreign wars, apart from conflict with the [Nguyễn lords](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_lords "Nguyễn lords") for control of [Cambodia](/wiki/Cambodia "Cambodia") starting around 1715. The last fifty years of the kingdom witnessed bloody succession crises, where there were purges of court officials and able generals for many consecutive reigns. In 1765, a combined 40,000-strong force of Burmese armies [invaded it](/wiki/Burmese%E2%80%93Siamese_War_(1759%E2%80%9360) "Burmese–Siamese War (1759–60)") from the north and west.[[90]](#cite_note-98): 250 The Burmese under the new [Alaungpaya](/wiki/Alaungpaya "Alaungpaya") dynasty quickly rose to become a new local power by 1759. After a 14-month siege, the capital city's walls fell and the city was burned in April 1767.[[91]](#cite_note-99): 218

### Thonburi Kingdom (1767–1782)

Main article: [Thonburi Kingdom](/wiki/Thonburi_Kingdom "Thonburi Kingdom")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/KingTaksin%27s_coronation.jpg/250px-KingTaksin%27s_coronation.jpg)](/wiki/File:KingTaksin%27s_coronation.jpg)

[Taksin the Great](/wiki/Taksin_the_Great "Taksin the Great") enthroned himself as a Thai king in 1767.

The capital and many of its territories lay in chaos after the war. The former capital was occupied by the [Burmese](/wiki/Burma "Burma") garrison army and five local leaders declared themselves overlords, including the lords of Sakwangburi, [Phitsanulok](/wiki/Phitsanulok "Phitsanulok"), [Pimai](/wiki/Pimai "Pimai"), [Chanthaburi](/wiki/Chanthaburi "Chanthaburi"), and [Nakhon Si Thammarat](/wiki/Nakhon_Si_Thammarat "Nakhon Si Thammarat"). [Chao Tak](/wiki/Taksin "Taksin"), a capable military leader, proceeded to make himself a lord by [right of conquest](/wiki/Right_of_conquest "Right of conquest"), beginning with the legendary sack of [Chanthaburi](/wiki/Chanthaburi "Chanthaburi"). Based at Chanthaburi, Chao Tak raised troops and resources, and sent a fleet up the [Chao Phraya](/wiki/Chao_Phraya "Chao Phraya") to take the fort of [Thonburi](/wiki/Thonburi "Thonburi"). In the same year, Chao Tak was able to retake Ayutthaya from the Burmese only seven months after the fall of the city.[[92]](#cite_note-100)

Chao Tak then crowned himself as [Taksin](/wiki/Taksin "Taksin") and proclaimed [Thonburi](/wiki/Thonburi "Thonburi") as temporary capital in the same year. He also quickly subdued the other warlords. His forces engaged in wars with Burma, Laos, and Cambodia, which successfully [drove the Burmese out of Lan Na](/wiki/Burmese%E2%80%93Siamese_War_(1774%E2%80%931775) "Burmese–Siamese War (1774–1775)") in 1775,[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 225 [captured Vientiane](/wiki/Lao%E2%80%93Siamese_War_(1778%E2%80%931779) "Lao–Siamese War (1778–1779)") in 1778[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 227–8 and [tried to install a pro-Thai king in Cambodia](/wiki/Siamese%E2%80%93Vietnamese_War_(1769%E2%80%931773) "Siamese–Vietnamese War (1769–1773)") in the 1770s. In his final years there was a coup, supposedly caused by his "insanity", and eventually Taksin and his sons were executed by his longtime companion General [Chao Phraya Chakri](/wiki/Rama_I "Rama I") (the future Rama I). He was the first king of the ruling [Chakri dynasty](/wiki/Chakri_dynasty "Chakri dynasty") and founder of the [Rattanakosin Kingdom](/wiki/Rattanakosin_Kingdom "Rattanakosin Kingdom") on 6 April 1782.[[93]](#cite_note-101)

### Rattanakosin Kingdom and modernisation (1782–1932)

Main article: [Rattanakosin Kingdom](/wiki/Rattanakosin_Kingdom_(1782%E2%80%931932) "Rattanakosin Kingdom (1782–1932)")

[Rattanakosin Kingdom](/wiki/Rattanakosin_Kingdom "Rattanakosin Kingdom")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Siam_in_1900%2C_During_Rama_V%27s_Reign.jpg/330px-Siam_in_1900%2C_During_Rama_V%27s_Reign.jpg)](/wiki/File:Siam_in_1900,_During_Rama_V%27s_Reign.jpg)

Detailed map of Siam's provinces, vassals, and monthons in 1900

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Emerald_Buddha%2C_August_2012%2C_Bangkok_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Emerald_Buddha%2C_August_2012%2C_Bangkok_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Emerald_Buddha,_August_2012,_Bangkok_(cropped).jpg)

[Emerald Buddha](/wiki/Emerald_Buddha "Emerald Buddha") in [Wat Phra Kaew](/wiki/Wat_Phra_Kaew "Wat Phra Kaew"). Considered the sacred palladium of Thailand.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/King_Chulalongkorn_and_Tsar_Nicholas_II_1897.png/120px-King_Chulalongkorn_and_Tsar_Nicholas_II_1897.png)](/wiki/File:King_Chulalongkorn_and_Tsar_Nicholas_II_1897.png)

[Chulalongkorn](/wiki/Chulalongkorn "Chulalongkorn") with [Nicholas II](/wiki/Nicholas_II "Nicholas II") in Saint Petersburg, 1897

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Siamese_Territory_and_Dominion_in_1805.jpg/330px-Siamese_Territory_and_Dominion_in_1805.jpg)](/wiki/File:Siamese_Territory_and_Dominion_in_1805.jpg)

Siamese territory and dominion at its largest in 1805, resulting from the [Burmese–Siamese War (1802–1805)](/wiki/Burmese%E2%80%93Siamese_War_(1802%E2%80%931805) "Burmese–Siamese War (1802–1805)")

Under [Rama I](/wiki/Rama_I "Rama I") (1782–1809), Rattanakosin successfully defended against Burmese attacks and put an end to Burmese incursions. He also created suzerainty over large portions of Laos and Cambodia.[[94]](#cite_note-102) In 1821, Briton [John Crawfurd](/wiki/John_Crawfurd "John Crawfurd") was sent to negotiate a new trade agreement with Siam – the first sign of an issue which was to dominate 19th century Siamese politics.[[95]](#cite_note-103) Bangkok signed the [Burney Treaty](/wiki/Burney_Treaty "Burney Treaty") in 1826, after the British victory in the [First Anglo-Burmese War](/wiki/First_Anglo-Burmese_War "First Anglo-Burmese War").[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 281

[Anouvong](/wiki/Anouvong "Anouvong") of Vientiane, who mistakenly held the belief that Britain was about to launch an invasion of Bangkok, started the [Lao rebellion](/wiki/Lao_rebellion_(1826%E2%80%9328) "Lao rebellion (1826–28)") in 1826, which was suppressed.[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 283–5 Vientiane was destroyed and a large number of [Lao people](/wiki/Lao_people "Lao people") were relocated to [Khorat Plateau](/wiki/Khorat_Plateau "Khorat Plateau") as a result.[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 285–6 Bangkok also waged [several wars](/wiki/Siamese%E2%80%93Vietnamese_wars "Siamese–Vietnamese wars") with [Vietnam](/wiki/T%C3%A2y_S%C6%A1n_dynasty "Tây Sơn dynasty"), where Siam successfully regained hegemony over Cambodia.[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 290–2

From the late 19th century, Siam tried to rule the ethnic groups in the realm as colonies.[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 308 In the reign of [Mongkut](/wiki/Mongkut "Mongkut") (1851–1868), who recognised the potential threat Western powers posed to Siam, his court contacted the [British government](/wiki/Government_of_the_United_Kingdom "Government of the United Kingdom") directly to defuse tensions.[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 311 A British mission led by Sir [John Bowring](/wiki/John_Bowring "John Bowring"), Governor of [Hong Kong](/wiki/British_Hong_Kong "British Hong Kong"), led to the signing of the [Bowring Treaty](/wiki/Bowring_Treaty "Bowring Treaty"), the first of many [unequal treaties](/wiki/Unequal_treaty "Unequal treaty") with Western countries. This, however, brought trade and economic development to Siam.[[96]](#cite_note-104) The unexpected death of Mongkut from [malaria](/wiki/Malaria "Malaria") led to the reign of underage King [Chulalongkorn](/wiki/Chulalongkorn "Chulalongkorn"), with [Somdet Chaophraya Sri Suriwongse](/wiki/Somdet_Chaophraya_Sri_Suriwongse "Somdet Chaophraya Sri Suriwongse") (Chuang Bunnag) acting as regent.[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 327

Chulalongkorn (r. 1868–1910) initiated centralisation, set up a privy council, and abolished slavery and the [corvée](/wiki/Corv%C3%A9e "Corvée") system. The [Front Palace crisis](/wiki/Front_Palace_crisis "Front Palace crisis") of 1874 stalled attempts at further reforms.[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 331–3 In the 1870s and 1880s, he incorporated the protectorates up north into the kingdom proper, which later expanded to the protectorates in the northeast and the south.[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 334–5 He established twelve *krom* in 1888, which were equivalent to present-day ministries.[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 347 The [crisis of 1893](/wiki/Franco-Siamese_War "Franco-Siamese War") erupted, caused by French demands for Laotian territory east of Mekong.[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 350–3

Thailand is the only Southeast Asian state never to have been colonised by a Western power,[[97]](#cite_note-105) in part because Britain and France agreed in 1896 to make the [Chao Phraya](/wiki/Chao_Phraya "Chao Phraya") valley a [buffer state](/wiki/Buffer_state "Buffer state").[[98]](#cite_note-106) Not until the 20th century could Siam renegotiate every unequal treaty dating from the Bowring Treaty, including [extraterritoriality](/wiki/Extraterritoriality "Extraterritoriality"). The advent of the *monthon* system marked the creation of the modern Thai nation-state.[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 362–3 In 1905, there were unsuccessful rebellions in the ancient [Patani](/wiki/Patani_(historical_region) "Patani (historical region)") area, Ubon Ratchathani, and Phrae in opposition to an attempt to blunt the power of local lords.[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 371–3

The [Palace Revolt of 1912](/wiki/Palace_Revolt_of_1912 "Palace Revolt of 1912") was a failed attempt by Western-educated military officers to overthrow the Siamese monarchy.[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 397 [Vajiravudh](/wiki/Vajiravudh "Vajiravudh") (r. 1910–1925) responded by propaganda for the entirety of his reign,[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 402 which promoted the [idea of the Thai nation](/wiki/Thaification "Thaification").[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 404 In 1917, Siam [joined the First World War](/wiki/Siam_in_World_War_I "Siam in World War I") on the side of [the Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I").[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 407 In the aftermath, Siam had a seat at the [Paris Peace Conference](/wiki/Paris_Peace_Conference,_1919 "Paris Peace Conference, 1919") and gained freedom of taxation and the revocation of extraterritoriality.[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 408

### Military dictatorship era (1932–1973)

Main articles: [Thailand in World War II](/wiki/Thailand_in_World_War_II "Thailand in World War II") and [History of Thailand (1932–1973)](/wiki/History_of_Thailand_(1932%E2%80%931973) "History of Thailand (1932–1973)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Field_Marshal_Plaek_Phibunsongkhram.jpg/250px-Field_Marshal_Plaek_Phibunsongkhram.jpg)](/wiki/File:Field_Marshal_Plaek_Phibunsongkhram.jpg)

Field Marshal Plaek Pibulsonggram

A period of wasteful spending by [King Vajiravudh](/wiki/Vajiravudh "Vajiravudh") followed by the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") brought economic hardships on Thailand, with sharply falling rice prices, a significant reduction in public spending, eroded confidence in the monarchy, and discontent among aristocratic supporters all contributing to the destabilisation of the [Chakri dynasty](/wiki/Chakri_dynasty "Chakri dynasty"). These conditions spurred on the bloodless [Siamese revolution of 1932](/wiki/Siamese_revolution_of_1932 "Siamese revolution of 1932"), in which the [Khana Ratsadon](/wiki/Khana_Ratsadon "Khana Ratsadon") movement forced [King Prajadhipok](/wiki/Prajadhipok "Prajadhipok") to sign the country's first constitution, thereby ending centuries of feudal and [absolute monarchy](/wiki/Absolute_monarchy "Absolute monarchy").[[26]](#cite_note-LePoer_1989-33): 25 Power was partially ceded to a [democratic](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy") government, but it would not last a year before the rise of significant military influence in Thai political affairs.

In 1933, [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Thailand "Prime Minister of Thailand") [Phraya Mano](/wiki/Phraya_Manopakorn_Nitithada "Phraya Manopakorn Nitithada") led a [counter-revolutionary rebellion](/wiki/Boworadet_rebellion "Boworadet rebellion") to reinstate an absolute monarchy under Prajadhipok.[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 446–8 This was soon followed by a military coup that installed [Phraya Phahon](/wiki/Phraya_Phahonphonphayuhasena "Phraya Phahonphonphayuhasena") – a leader of the 1932 revolution – as Prime Minister. Prajadhipok's conflict with the government eventually led to his abdication, and the new military-led government selected [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland")-raised [Ananda Mahidol](/wiki/Ananda_Mahidol "Ananda Mahidol") to be the new king.[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 448–9

The armed wing of Khana Ratsadon gradually came to dominate Siamese politics. Military strongman [Plaek Phibunsongkhram](/wiki/Plaek_Phibunsongkhram "Plaek Phibunsongkhram"), often known as "Phibun", became premier in 1938 and launched a [campaign of political repression](/wiki/Songsuradet_rebellion "Songsuradet rebellion") that saw the execution of both Monarchists and pro-democracy [Liberals](/wiki/Liberalism "Liberalism").[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 457 His government adopted [Thai nationalist](/wiki/Thai_nationalism "Thai nationalism"), pro-[Westernisation](/wiki/Westernisation "Westernisation"), anti-royalist, [Irredentist](/wiki/Irredentism "Irredentism"), [anti-Chinese](/wiki/Sinophobia "Sinophobia") and anti-French policies.[[26]](#cite_note-LePoer_1989-33): 28

In 1939, Phibun changed the country's name from "Siam" to "Thailand". This coincided with the rise of [fascist ideology](/wiki/Fascism "Fascism") in Thailand, with the name change reflecting policies that prioritised the needs of [ethnic Thais](/wiki/Tai_peoples "Tai peoples") over those of minorities like the [Thai Chinese](/wiki/Thai_Chinese "Thai Chinese"). Phibun was an admirer of [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini"), and he imposed twelve [Thai cultural mandates](/wiki/Thai_cultural_mandates "Thai cultural mandates") requiring citizens to obey nationalist norms such as saluting the flag, knowing the national anthem, speaking only Thai, and wearing Western clothing.

In October 1940, months after [fall of France](/wiki/Battle_of_France "Battle of France") to [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany"), Phibun launched an opportunistic [invasion](/wiki/Franco-Thai_War "Franco-Thai War") of [French Indochinese](/wiki/French_Indochina "French Indochina") territories [lost](/wiki/Territorial_losses_of_Thailand "Territorial losses of Thailand") to the [French Empire](/wiki/France "France") during the reign of [King Chulalongkorn](/wiki/Chulalongkorn "Chulalongkorn"). The invasion was a success, with Thailand additionally gaining some [Lao](/wiki/Laos "Laos") and [Cambodian](/wiki/Cambodia "Cambodia") territories.[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 462Japan, which had also been invading French Indochinese territories, brokered a ceasefire that forced France to give up several territories to Thailand.

On 8 December 1941, [the Japanese Empire launched an invasion of Thailand](/wiki/Japanese_invasion_of_Thailand "Japanese invasion of Thailand"), and fierce fighting broke out in Southern Thailand before Phibun ordered an [armistice](/wiki/Armistice "Armistice") and joined the [Axis powers](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers") just five hours later. Japan was granted free passage through Thai territory, and on 21 December Thailand and Japan signed a military alliance, with a secret protocol wherein the Japanese government agreed to help Thailand gain further territories throughout southeast Asia.[[99]](#cite_note-107)

The Thai government then declared war on the United States and the United Kingdom.[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 465 The United Kingdom, whose colony [Malaya](/wiki/British_Malaya "British Malaya") was under immediate threat from Thai forces, responded in kind, but the United States refused to declare war and ignored Thailand's declaration.[[100]](#cite_note-108): 66 The [Free Thai Movement](/wiki/Free_Thai_Movement "Free Thai Movement") was launched both in Thailand and abroad to oppose the Thai government and Japanese occupation.[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 465–6 After the war ended in 1945, Phibun was ousted and Thailand signed formal agreements to end the state of war with [the Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II"). In June 1946, King Ananda was found dead under mysterious circumstances, and his younger brother [Bhumibol Adulyadej](/wiki/Bhumibol_Adulyadej "Bhumibol Adulyadej") ascended to the throne.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/ATrelations0018a-1.jpg/250px-ATrelations0018a-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:ATrelations0018a-1.jpg)

King [Bhumibol Adulyadej](/wiki/Bhumibol_Adulyadej "Bhumibol Adulyadej") addresses a [joint session of the United States Congress](/wiki/Joint_session_of_the_United_States_Congress "Joint session of the United States Congress"), 1960.

This period of Liberal rule was brief, with Phibun launching another military coup to take power in 1947. In his second term as Prime Minister, Phibun pursued an alliance with the United States by undertaking a campaign of domestic [anti-communism](/wiki/Anti-communism "Anti-communism"), aiding the [US invasion of Korea](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War"), and helping to establish the US-aligned [Southeast Asia Treaty Organization](/wiki/Southeast_Asia_Treaty_Organization "Southeast Asia Treaty Organization") (SEATO) in 1954.[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 493 Following a tour of Europe and the US in 1955 where he was impressed by democratic practices like [freedom of speech](/wiki/Freedom_of_speech_in_the_United_States "Freedom of speech in the United States"), Phibun began to democratise Thai politics by allowing new political parties and trade unions to form, opening public forums, granting amnesty to former political opponents, and preparing Thailand for its first [free elections](/wiki/Election "Election"). However, these practices likely aroused opposition to Phibun's dictatorial legacy, and the US supported his final ouster in 1957.[[101]](#cite_note-109)

Field Marshal [Sarit Thanarat](/wiki/Sarit_Thanarat "Sarit Thanarat") took power by coup in 1957, and he proceeded to reverse Phibun's democratic reforms and ban the Khana Ratsadon party from politics. His rule from 1959 to 1963 was autocratic; he built his legitimacy around the god-like status of the monarch and by channelling the government and public's loyalty to the king.[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 511 Though Phibun had been criticised for his close relationship to the United States, Sarit also aligned himself with them and militarily supported the [United States invasion of Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War") in 1961, leading a simultaneous campaign of [anti-communist mass killings](/wiki/Anti-communist_mass_killings#Thailand "Anti-communist mass killings") in Thailand that continued under his successor [Thanom Kittikachorn](/wiki/Thanom_Kittikachorn "Thanom Kittikachorn").[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 523

The Sarit and Thanom periods coincided with significant [modernisation](/wiki/Modernisation "Modernisation") and [Westernisation](/wiki/Westernisation "Westernisation") of Thai society. Rapid [urbanisation](/wiki/Urbanisation "Urbanisation") occurred as the rural populace sought work in growing cities like [Bangkok](/wiki/Bangkok "Bangkok"). Economic development and public education enabled the rise of an urban middle class.[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 534 The growth of this economy based on [wage labour](/wiki/Wage_labour "Wage labour") rather than [subsistence agriculture](/wiki/Subsistence_agriculture "Subsistence agriculture") resulted in increased [class consciousness](/wiki/Class_consciousness "Class consciousness") and sympathies to the [Communist Party of Thailand](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Thailand "Communist Party of Thailand"),[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 528 while university-educated students formed movements against the military dictatorship.

### Transition toward democracy (1973–2001)

Main article: [History of Thailand (1973–2001)](/wiki/History_of_Thailand_(1973%E2%80%932001) "History of Thailand (1973–2001)")

In October 1973, a [popular democratic uprising](/wiki/1973_Thai_popular_uprising "1973 Thai popular uprising") against the Thanom government began to bring about an end to Thailand's military regime.[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 541–3 In response to mass killings of protesters, King Bhumibol installed [Sanya Dharmasakti](/wiki/Sanya_Dharmasakti "Sanya Dharmasakti") as Prime Minister, marking the first time that the king had intervened in Thai politics directly since 1932.[[102]](#cite_note-Britannica2-110) The aftermath of the event marked a short-lived parliamentary democracy,[[102]](#cite_note-Britannica2-110) often called the "era when democracy blossomed" (ยุคประชาธิปไตยเบ่งบาน).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

With new democratic institutions limited in their capability to solidify power and pass reforms, the three years after the uprising were marked by political and economic instability. Right-wing fears of a Communist takeover of Thailand grew following the captures of South Vietnam, Cambodia, and Laos by Communist insurgents. These factors culminated in the [Thammasat University massacre](/wiki/6_October_1976_massacre "6 October 1976 massacre") in October 1976, in which police and right-wing mobs massacred students protesting the return of Thanom Kittikachorn to Thai politics.[[88]](#cite_note-Wyatt_20132-96): 548–9 A military coup d'état on that day brought Thailand a new ultra-[right-wing](/wiki/Right-wing_politics "Right-wing politics") military government which cracked down on media outlets, government officials, and intellectuals, engendering a [Communist insurgency](/wiki/Communist_insurgency_in_Thailand "Communist insurgency in Thailand"). Another military coup the following year installed a more moderate military government, which offered amnesty to some Communist fighters in 1978.[[103]](#cite_note-111)

Military figure [Prem Tinsulanonda](/wiki/Prem_Tinsulanonda "Prem Tinsulanonda") was appointed Prime Minister from in 1980. He oversaw the beginning of a period of significant economic growth[[104]](#cite_note-112) and democratisation throughout the 1980s. Prem's premiership was often referred to as "premocracy" because it involved [semi-democratic](/wiki/Semi-democracy "Semi-democracy") practices such as a Parliament composed of an all-elected House and an all-appointed Senate.[[105]](#cite_note-113) Prem's [constitutionalist](/wiki/Constitutionalism "Constitutionalism") political approach aided with a transition away from military rule and a negotiated end to the Communist insurgency by 1983.[[106]](#cite_note-114) He maintained political stability via a close relationship with [King Bhumibol](/wiki/Bhumibol_Adulyadej "Bhumibol Adulyadej"), who helped to foil two military coups in attempts against Prem in [1981](/wiki/1981_Thai_military_rebellion "1981 Thai military rebellion") and in [1985](/wiki/1985_Thai_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat_attempt "1985 Thai coup d'état attempt").

In 1988, Thailand saw its first freely-elected prime minister since the 1976 coup. [Chatichai Choonhavan](/wiki/Chatichai_Choonhavan "Chatichai Choonhavan") ruled for 3 years before being overthrown in a coup that ushered in two years of military rule under the [National Peace Keeping Council](/wiki/National_Peace_Keeping_Council "National Peace Keeping Council").[[107]](#cite_note-115) One of the coup leaders, [Suchinda Kraprayoon](/wiki/Suchinda_Kraprayoon "Suchinda Kraprayoon"), was nominated as prime minister for the majority coalition government after the [March 1992 general election](/wiki/March_1992_Thai_general_election "March 1992 Thai general election"). This caused a popular demonstration in Bangkok, which ended with a bloody military crackdown known as [Black May](/wiki/Black_May_(1992) "Black May (1992)").[[108]](#cite_note-116) The King intervened in the event, granting amnesty to the belligerents and forcing the resignation of Suchinda.[[109]](#cite_note-117)

Free elections were once again held in September 1992, bringing the [neoliberal](/wiki/Neoliberalism "Neoliberalism") [Democrat Party](/wiki/Democrat_Party_(Thailand) "Democrat Party (Thailand)") to power. Power passed between various neoliberal parties throughout the decade, who all had a hand in drafting the [1997 "People's Constitution."](/wiki/1997_constitution_of_Thailand "1997 constitution of Thailand") Spurred on by the events of Black May, the constitution explicitly acknowledged several [human rights](/wiki/Human_rights "Human rights") and established several government and electoral accountability measures, including the creation of a fully-elected [bicameral legislature](/wiki/Bicameral_legislature "Bicameral legislature").

### Shinawatra era (2001–present)

Main article: [History of Thailand (2001–present)](/wiki/History_of_Thailand_(2001%E2%80%93present) "History of Thailand (2001–present)")

The neoliberal government's attempt to [peg](/wiki/Currency_peg "Currency peg") the [Thai baht](/wiki/Thai_baht "Thai baht") to the [United States dollar](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar") induced the [1997 Asian financial crisis](/wiki/1997_Asian_financial_crisis "1997 Asian financial crisis"), crashing regional economies and ending nearly 40 years of uninterrupted economic growth in Thailand.[[110]](#cite_note-118): 3 To cover a shortage of foreign currency and investments, which had come primarily from Japan, [Chavalit Yongchaiyudh](/wiki/Chavalit_Yongchaiyudh "Chavalit Yongchaiyudh")'s government took out a $17 billion [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund") loan in US dollars.[[111]](#cite_note-119) To satisfy the conditions of the loan, Thailand underwent a series of [austerity measures](/wiki/Austerity "Austerity") until the loan was repaid in 2003.

The crisis brought on the ascendancy of the populist [Thai Rak Thai party](/wiki/Thai_Rak_Thai_party "Thai Rak Thai party"), whose base consisted primarily of indebted rural workers. Its prime minister [Thaksin Shinawatra](/wiki/Thaksin_Shinawatra "Thaksin Shinawatra") governed from 2001 until 2006. His policies were successful in reducing rural poverty[[112]](#cite_note-120) and providing [universal healthcare](/wiki/Universal_healthcare "Universal healthcare").[[113]](#cite_note-121) However, Thaksin was viewed by opponents among the Thai upper and upper middle classes as a corrupt populist who was destroying the middle class in order to favour himself and the rural poor. Additionally, his rural poverty policies directly conflicted with King Bhumibol's recommendations, drawing the ire of royalists, a powerful faction in Thailand. Thaksin also faced crises in the form of the [South Thailand insurgency](/wiki/Southern_Thailand_insurgency "Southern Thailand insurgency"), which escalated starting from 2004, and the [2004 Indian Ocean earthquake and tsunami](/wiki/2004_Indian_Ocean_earthquake_and_tsunami "2004 Indian Ocean earthquake and tsunami") which claimed around 8,000 lives in Thailand.[[114]](#cite_note-122)

Massive protests against Thaksin led by the [reactionary](/wiki/Reactionary "Reactionary") [People's Alliance for Democracy](/wiki/People%27s_Alliance_for_Democracy "People's Alliance for Democracy") (PAD) started in his second term as prime minister, and the monarchy and the military eventually agreed to oust him. The [crisis](/wiki/2005%E2%80%932006_Thai_political_crisis "2005–2006 Thai political crisis") peaked in 2005 with a series of controversies surrounding Buddhist and monarchic institutions, and the army dissolved Thaksin's party with [a coup d'état in 2006](/wiki/2006_Thai_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "2006 Thai coup d'état") and banned over a hundred of its executives from politics. After the coup, a military government was installed for one year.**[[115]](#cite_note-Phongpaichit-2008-123)**[[116]](#cite_note-124)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/2010_09_19_red_shirt_protest_bkk_09.JPG/250px-2010_09_19_red_shirt_protest_bkk_09.JPG)](/wiki/File:2010_09_19_red_shirt_protest_bkk_09.JPG)

[United Front for Democracy Against Dictatorship](/wiki/United_Front_for_Democracy_Against_Dictatorship "United Front for Democracy Against Dictatorship"), Red Shirts, [protest](/wiki/2010_Thai_political_protests "2010 Thai political protests") in 2010

The military government faced significant resistance, with protesters frequently storming government buildings and elements within the military threatening yet another coup.**[[115]](#cite_note-Phongpaichit-2008-123)** In 2007, a civilian government led by the Thaksin-aligned [People's Power Party](/wiki/People%27s_Power_Party_(Thailand) "People's Power Party (Thailand)") (PPP) was [elected](/wiki/2007_Thai_general_election "2007 Thai general election"). [Another protest led by PAD](/wiki/2008_Thai_political_crisis "2008 Thai political crisis") ended with the dissolution of PPP, and the [Democrat Party](/wiki/Democrat_Party_(Thailand) "Democrat Party (Thailand)") led a coalition government in its place. The pro-Thaksin [United Front for Democracy Against Dictatorship](/wiki/United_Front_for_Democracy_Against_Dictatorship "United Front for Democracy Against Dictatorship") (UDD) protested both [in 2009](/wiki/2009_Thai_political_unrest "2009 Thai political unrest") and [in 2010](/wiki/2010_Thai_political_protests "2010 Thai political protests"), the latter of which ended with [a violent military crackdown](/wiki/2010_Thai_military_crackdown "2010 Thai military crackdown") causing more than 70 civilian deaths.[[117]](#cite_note-125)

After [the general election of 2011](/wiki/2011_Thai_general_election "2011 Thai general election"), the [populist](/wiki/Populism "Populism") [Pheu Thai Party](/wiki/Pheu_Thai_Party "Pheu Thai Party") won a majority. [Yingluck Shinawatra](/wiki/Yingluck_Shinawatra "Yingluck Shinawatra"), Thaksin's younger sister, became prime minister. The [People's Democratic Reform Committee](/wiki/People%27s_Democratic_Reform_Committee "People's Democratic Reform Committee") organised another anti-Shinawatra protest[[118]](#cite_note-126) after the ruling party proposed an amnesty bill which would benefit Thaksin.[[119]](#cite_note-127) Yingluck dissolved parliament and [a general election](/wiki/2014_Thai_general_election "2014 Thai general election") was scheduled, but her decision was invalidated by the [Constitutional Court](/wiki/Constitutional_Court_of_Thailand "Constitutional Court of Thailand"). The [crisis](/wiki/2013%E2%80%932014_Thai_political_crisis "2013–2014 Thai political crisis") ended with [another coup d'état in 2014](/wiki/2014_Thai_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "2014 Thai coup d'état").[[120]](#cite_note-128)
