The ensuing [National Council for Peace and Order](/wiki/National_Council_for_Peace_and_Order "National Council for Peace and Order"), a military junta led by General [Prayut Chan-o-cha](/wiki/Prayut_Chan-o-cha "Prayut Chan-o-cha"), led the country until 2019. Civil and political rights were restricted, and the country saw a surge in *[lèse-majesté](/wiki/L%C3%A8se-majest%C3%A9_in_Thailand "Lèse-majesté in Thailand")* cases. Political opponents and dissenters were sent to "attitude adjustment" camps;[[121]](#cite_note-Beech_20192-129) this was described by academics as indicating the return of fascism to Thailand.[[122]](#cite_note-130) Bhumibol, the longest-reigning Thai king, [died in 2016](/wiki/Death_and_funeral_of_Bhumibol_Adulyadej "Death and funeral of Bhumibol Adulyadej"), and his son [Vajiralongkorn](/wiki/Vajiralongkorn "Vajiralongkorn") ascended to the throne. The referendum and adoption of Thailand's current constitution happened under the junta's rule.[[ix]](#cite_note-132) The junta bound future governments to a 20-year national strategy 'road map' it laid down, effectively locking the country into [military-guided democracy](/wiki/Guided_democracy "Guided democracy").[[124]](#cite_note-133)

In 2019, the junta agreed to schedule [a general election](/wiki/2019_Thai_general_election "2019 Thai general election").[[121]](#cite_note-Beech_20192-129) General Prayut continued his premiership with the support of an elected [Palang Pracharath Party](/wiki/Palang_Pracharath_Party "Palang Pracharath Party") coalition in the House and junta-appointed Senate, despite allegations of election fraud.[[125]](#cite_note-134) An extended two-year period of [pro-democracy protests](/wiki/2020%E2%80%932021_Thai_protests "2020–2021 Thai protests") were subsequently triggered by distrust in the election as well as increasing [royal prerogative](/wiki/Royal_prerogative "Royal prerogative"), democratic and economic regression under monarchy-supported military rule, retaliatory dissolution of the pro-democracy [Future Forward Party](/wiki/Future_Forward_Party "Future Forward Party"), regressive policies on political issues like women's and labour rights, forced disappearances and deaths of political activists like [Wanchalearm Satsaksit](/wiki/Wanchalearm_Satsaksit "Wanchalearm Satsaksit"), and various political corruption scandals including [1MDB](/wiki/1Malaysia_Development_Berhad_scandal "1Malaysia Development Berhad scandal").[[126]](#cite_note-135)[[127]](#cite_note-136) The protests brought forward unprecedented demands to reform the monarchy[[128]](#cite_note-137) and the highest sense of [republicanism](/wiki/Republicanism "Republicanism") in the country's history.[[129]](#cite_note-138)

In May 2023, Thailand's reformist opposition – the progressive [Move Forward Party](/wiki/Move_Forward_Party "Move Forward Party") (MFP) and Thaksin's populist Pheu Thai Party – won the [general election](/wiki/2023_Thai_general_election "2023 Thai general election"), ending 9 years of royalist-military rule under Prayut.[[130]](#cite_note-139) On 22 August 2023, [Srettha Thavisin](/wiki/Srettha_Thavisin "Srettha Thavisin") of the Pheu Thai party became Thailand's new prime minister, while the ex-PM and party figurehead Thaksin Shinawatra returned to Thailand after years in self-imposed exile from the military regime.[[131]](#cite_note-140) Thavisin was later dismissed from his prime ministerial role on 14 August 2024 by the [Constitutional Court](/wiki/Constitutional_Court_of_Thailand "Constitutional Court of Thailand") for "gross ethics violations".[[132]](#cite_note-141) He was succeeded by Thaksin's daughter [Paetongtarn Shinawatra](/wiki/Paetongtarn_Shinawatra "Paetongtarn Shinawatra"), who spent a year in office before the Constitutional Court ordered her removal over a controversial phone call with Cambodia's president. The Court then ordered the arrest of Thaksin in September 2025.[[133]](#cite_note-142)

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of Thailand](/wiki/Geography_of_Thailand "Geography of Thailand")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Koh_Tarutao%2C_Thailand%2C_Ancient_Cambrian_deposits.jpg/250px-Koh_Tarutao%2C_Thailand%2C_Ancient_Cambrian_deposits.jpg)](/wiki/File:Koh_Tarutao,_Thailand,_Ancient_Cambrian_deposits.jpg)

[Ko Tarutao](/wiki/Ko_Tarutao "Ko Tarutao"), [Satun](/wiki/Satun_Province "Satun Province")

Totalling 513,120 square kilometres (198,120 sq mi), Thailand is the [50th-largest country](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_area "List of countries and dependencies by area") by total area.[[1]](#cite_note-CIA-2) Thailand comprises several distinct geographic regions, partly corresponding to the provincial groups. The north of the country is the mountainous area of the [Thai highlands](/wiki/Thai_highlands "Thai highlands"), with the highest point being [Doi Inthanon](/wiki/Doi_Inthanon "Doi Inthanon") in the [Thanon Thong Chai Range](/wiki/Thanon_Thong_Chai_Range "Thanon Thong Chai Range") at 2,565 metres (8,415 ft) above sea level. The northeast, [Isan](/wiki/Isan "Isan"), consists of the [Khorat Plateau](/wiki/Khorat_Plateau "Khorat Plateau"), bordered to the east by the [Mekong River](/wiki/Mekong_River "Mekong River"). The centre of the country is dominated by the predominantly flat [Chao Phraya](/wiki/Chao_Phraya "Chao Phraya") river valley, which runs into the [Gulf of Thailand](/wiki/Gulf_of_Thailand "Gulf of Thailand"). Southern Thailand consists of the narrow [Kra Isthmus](/wiki/Kra_Isthmus "Kra Isthmus") that widens into the [Malay Peninsula](/wiki/Malay_Peninsula "Malay Peninsula").

The Chao Phraya and the Mekong River are the indispensable water courses of rural Thailand. Industrial scale production of crops use both rivers and their tributaries. The Gulf of Thailand covers 320,000 square kilometres (124,000 sq mi) and is fed by the Chao Phraya, [Mae Klong](/wiki/Mae_Klong_River "Mae Klong River"), [Bang Pakong](/wiki/Bang_Pakong_River "Bang Pakong River"), and [Tapi](/wiki/Tapi_River,_Thailand "Tapi River, Thailand") Rivers. It contributes to the tourism sector owing to its clear shallow waters along the coasts in the southern region and the Kra Isthmus. The eastern shore of the Gulf of Thailand has the kingdom's premier deepwater port in [Sattahip](/wiki/Sattahip_District "Sattahip District") and its busiest commercial port, [Laem Chabang](/wiki/Laem_Chabang "Laem Chabang"). [Phuket](/wiki/Phuket_Province "Phuket Province"), [Krabi](/wiki/Krabi_Province "Krabi Province"), [Ranong](/wiki/Ranong "Ranong"), [Phang Nga](/wiki/Phang_Nga "Phang Nga") and [Trang](/wiki/Trang_Province "Trang Province"), and their islands, all lay along the coasts of the [Andaman Sea](/wiki/Andaman_Sea "Andaman Sea").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Climate

Further information: [Climate change in Thailand](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Thailand "Climate change in Thailand")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Koppen-Geiger_Map_THA_present.svg/250px-Koppen-Geiger_Map_THA_present.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Koppen-Geiger_Map_THA_present.svg)

Thailand map of Köppen climate classification

Thailand's climate is influenced by monsoon winds that have a seasonal character (the southwest and northeast monsoon).[[134]](#cite_note-Climate_2015-143): 2 Most of the country is classified as [Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification")'s [tropical savanna climate](/wiki/Tropical_savanna_climate "Tropical savanna climate").[[135]](#cite_note-144) The majority of the south, as well as the eastern tip, have a [tropical monsoon climate](/wiki/Tropical_monsoon_climate "Tropical monsoon climate"). Parts of the south also have a [tropical rainforest climate](/wiki/Tropical_rainforest_climate "Tropical rainforest climate").

A year in Thailand is divided into three seasons.[[134]](#cite_note-Climate_2015-143): 2 The first is the rainy or southwest monsoon season (mid–May to mid–October), which is caused by southwestern wind from the [Indian Ocean](/wiki/Indian_Ocean "Indian Ocean").[[134]](#cite_note-Climate_2015-143): 2

Rainfall is also contributed by [Intertropical Convergence Zone](/wiki/Intertropical_Convergence_Zone "Intertropical Convergence Zone") (ITCZ) and tropical [cyclones](/wiki/Cyclones "Cyclones"),[[134]](#cite_note-Climate_2015-143): 2 with August and September being the wettest period of the year.[[134]](#cite_note-Climate_2015-143): 2 The country receives a mean annual rainfall of 1,200 to 1,600 mm (47 to 63 in).[[134]](#cite_note-Climate_2015-143): 4

Winter or the northeast monsoon occurs from mid-October until mid-February.[[134]](#cite_note-Climate_2015-143): 2 Most of Thailand experiences dry weather with mild temperatures.[[134]](#cite_note-Climate_2015-143): 2, 4 Summer or the pre-monsoon season runs from mid-February until mid-May.[[134]](#cite_note-Climate_2015-143): 3

Due to their inland position and latitude, the north, northeast, central and eastern parts of Thailand experience a long period of warm weather, where temperatures can reach up to 40 °C (104 °F) during March to May,[[134]](#cite_note-Climate_2015-143): 3 in contrast to close to or below 0 °C (32 °F) in some areas in winter.[[134]](#cite_note-Climate_2015-143): 3

Southern Thailand is characterised by mild weather year-round with less diurnal and seasonal variations in temperatures due to maritime influences.[[134]](#cite_note-Climate_2015-143): 3 It receives abundant rainfall, particularly during October to November.[[134]](#cite_note-Climate_2015-143): 2 Thailand is among the world's ten countries that are most exposed to climate change. In particular, it is highly vulnerable to [rising sea levels](/wiki/Sea_level_rise "Sea level rise") and extreme weather events.[[136]](#cite_note-145)[[137]](#cite_note-146)

### Biodiversity and conservation

See also: [List of protected areas of Thailand](/wiki/List_of_protected_areas_of_Thailand "List of protected areas of Thailand"), [List of forest parks of Thailand](/wiki/List_of_forest_parks_of_Thailand "List of forest parks of Thailand"), [List of species native to Thailand](/wiki/List_of_species_native_to_Thailand "List of species native to Thailand"), and [Environmental issues in Thailand](/wiki/Environmental_issues_in_Thailand "Environmental issues in Thailand")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Khaoyai_06.jpg/250px-Khaoyai_06.jpg)](/wiki/File:Khaoyai_06.jpg)

The population of [Asian elephants](/wiki/Asian_elephants "Asian elephants") in Thailand's wild has dropped to an estimated 2,000–3,000.[[138]](#cite_note-Elephants-147)

[National parks](/wiki/List_of_national_parks_of_Thailand "List of national parks of Thailand") in Thailand are defined as *an area that contains natural resources of ecological importance or unique beauty, or flora and fauna of special importance*. Thailand's protected areas include 156 national parks, 58 wildlife sanctuaries, 67 non-hunting areas, and 120 forest parks. They cover almost 31 per cent of the kingdom's territory.[[139]](#cite_note-PRD-148) The parks are administered by the [National Parks, Wildlife and Plant Conservation Department](/wiki/Department_of_National_Parks,_Wildlife_and_Plant_Conservation "Department of National Parks, Wildlife and Plant Conservation") (DNP) of the [Ministry of Natural Resources and Environment](/wiki/Ministry_of_Natural_Resources_and_Environment_(Thailand) "Ministry of Natural Resources and Environment (Thailand)") (MNRE). The [Khao Kheow Open Zoo](/wiki/Khao_Kheow_Open_Zoo "Khao Kheow Open Zoo") in [Chonburi](/wiki/Chonburi "Chonburi") is the second largest zoo in Asia.[[140]](#cite_note-cnbctv1-149) The largest national park of Thailand is [Kaeng Krachan National Park](/wiki/Kaeng_Krachan_National_Park "Kaeng Krachan National Park").

Thailand has a mediocre but improving performance in the global [Environmental Performance Index](/wiki/Environmental_Performance_Index "Environmental Performance Index") (EPI), with an overall ranking of 91 out of 180 countries in 2016. The environmental areas where Thailand performs worst (i.e., highest-ranking) are [air quality](/wiki/Air_quality "Air quality") (167), environmental effects of the agricultural industry (106), and the [climate and energy](/wiki/Climate_and_energy "Climate and energy") sector (93), the later mainly because of a high [CO2 emission](/wiki/CO2_emission "CO2 emission") per kWh produced. Thailand performs best (i.e., lowest-ranking) in [water resource management](/wiki/Water_resource_management "Water resource management") (66), with some major improvements expected for the future, and [sanitation](/wiki/Sanitation "Sanitation") (68).[[141]](#cite_note-150)[[142]](#cite_note-151) The country had a 2019 [Forest Landscape Integrity Index](/wiki/Forest_Landscape_Integrity_Index "Forest Landscape Integrity Index") mean score of 6.00/10, ranking it 88th globally out of 172 countries.[[143]](#cite_note-152)

The population of elephants, the country's [national symbol](/wiki/List_of_national_animals "List of national animals"), has fallen from 100,000 in 1850 to an estimated 2,000.[[138]](#cite_note-Elephants-147) Poachers have long hunted elephants for [ivory](/wiki/Ivory "Ivory") and hides, and now increasingly for [meat](/wiki/Elephant_meat "Elephant meat").[[144]](#cite_note-153) Young elephants are often captured for use in tourist attractions or as work animals, where there have been claims of mistreatment.[[145]](#cite_note-154) In 1989, the government banned the use of elephants for [logging](/wiki/Logging "Logging"), leading many elephant owners to move their domesticated animals to the tourism industry.[[146]](#cite_note-155)

Poaching of protected species remains a major problem. [Tigers](/wiki/Tigers "Tigers"), [leopards](/wiki/Leopards "Leopards"), and other large cats are hunted for their pelts. Many are farmed or hunted for their meat, which supposedly has medicinal properties. Although such trade is illegal, the well-known Bangkok market [Chatuchak](/wiki/Chatuchak_Weekend_Market "Chatuchak Weekend Market") is still known for the sale of endangered species.[[147]](#cite_note-156) The practice of keeping wild animals as pets affects species such as [Asiatic black bear](/wiki/Asiatic_black_bear "Asiatic black bear"), [Malayan sun bear](/wiki/Malayan_sun_bear "Malayan sun bear"), [white-handed lar](/wiki/Lar_gibbon "Lar gibbon"), [pileated gibbon](/wiki/Pileated_gibbon "Pileated gibbon"), and [binturong](/wiki/Binturong "Binturong").[[148]](#cite_note-157)

## Politics and government

Main articles: [Politics of Thailand](/wiki/Politics_of_Thailand "Politics of Thailand") and [Government of Thailand](/wiki/Government_of_Thailand "Government of Thailand")

Further information: [Constitutions of Thailand](/wiki/Constitutions_of_Thailand "Constitutions of Thailand") and [Elections in Thailand](/wiki/Elections_in_Thailand "Elections in Thailand")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/King_Rama_X_official_%28crop%29_%28cropped%29.png/250px-King_Rama_X_official_%28crop%29_%28cropped%29.png)](/wiki/File:King_Rama_X_official_(crop)_(cropped).png)

[Vajiralongkorn](/wiki/Vajiralongkorn "Vajiralongkorn")  
[King of Thailand](/wiki/King_of_Thailand "King of Thailand")  
since 13 October 2016

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Anutin_Charnvirakul_in_2025.jpg/250px-Anutin_Charnvirakul_in_2025.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anutin_Charnvirakul_in_2025.jpg)

[Anutin Charnvirakul](/wiki/Anutin_Charnvirakul "Anutin Charnvirakul")  
[Prime Minister of Thailand](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Thailand "Prime Minister of Thailand")  
since 7 September 2025

Prior to 1932, Thai kings were feudal or [absolute monarchs](/wiki/Absolute_monarchy "Absolute monarchy"). During [Sukhothai Kingdom](/wiki/Sukhothai_Kingdom "Sukhothai Kingdom"), the king was seen as a *Dharmaraja* or 'king who rules in accordance with [Dharma](/wiki/Dharma "Dharma")'. The system of government was a network of tributaries ruled by local lords. Modern absolute monarchy and statehood was established by Chulalongkorn when he transformed the decentralised protectorate system into a unitary state. On 24 June 1932, [Khana Ratsadon](/wiki/Khana_Ratsadon "Khana Ratsadon") (People's Party) carried out a bloodless revolution which marked the beginning of [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy").

Thailand has had 20 constitutions and charters since 1932, including the latest and current 2017 Constitution. All constitutions state that the politics is conducted within the framework of a constitutional monarchy, but the *de facto* form of government has ranged from military dictatorship to electoral democracy.[[149]](#cite_note-multiple-158)[[150]](#cite_note-159) Thailand's current form of government is part democracy and part dictatorship; many terms are used to describe it.[[x]](#cite_note-165) Thailand has had the fourth-most [coups](/wiki/Coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "Coup d'état") in the world.[[156]](#cite_note-166) "Uniformed or ex-military men have led Thailand for 55 of the 83 years" between 1932 and 2009.[[157]](#cite_note-167) Most recently, the [military junta](/wiki/Military_junta "Military junta") self-titled as the [National Council for Peace and Order](/wiki/National_Council_for_Peace_and_Order "National Council for Peace and Order") ruled the country between 2014 and 2019.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Sappaya-Sapasathan_Pano_%28III%29.jpg/250px-Sappaya-Sapasathan_Pano_%28III%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sappaya-Sapasathan_Pano_(III).jpg)

[Sappaya-Sapasathan](/wiki/Sappaya-Sapasathan "Sappaya-Sapasathan") is the current Parliament House of Thailand

Government is [separated](/wiki/Separation_of_powers "Separation of powers") into three branches:

* The legislative branch: the [National Assembly](/wiki/National_Assembly_of_Thailand "National Assembly of Thailand") is composed of the [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Thailand "Senate of Thailand"), the 200-member indirectly elected [upper house](/wiki/Upper_house "Upper house") and [House of Representatives](/wiki/House_of_Representatives_of_Thailand "House of Representatives of Thailand"), the elected 500-member [lower house](/wiki/Lower_house "Lower house"). Its most recent election is [the 2023 general election](/wiki/2023_Thai_general_election "2023 Thai general election"). The coalition led by Pheu Thai Party currently holds the majority. The [2024 Thai Senate election](/wiki/2024_Thai_Senate_election "2024 Thai Senate election") was the first senate election held under the current constitution in the process criticised as "the most complicated election in the world".[[158]](#cite_note-168) The senate is allegedly dominated by [Bhumjaithai Party](/wiki/Bhumjaithai_Party "Bhumjaithai Party")-affiliated senators.[[159]](#cite_note-169)
* The executive branch consisting of the [Prime Minister of Thailand](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Thailand "Prime Minister of Thailand"), the [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government"), and other cabinet members of up to 35 people. The Prime Minister was elected by the House of Representatives. The current constitution mandates that prime ministers are to be considered from candidates nominated by political parties before the election. The current prime minister is [Anutin Charnvirakul](/wiki/Anutin_Charnvirakul "Anutin Charnvirakul"), a member of the Bhumjaithai Party.
* The [judiciary](/wiki/Judiciary "Judiciary") is supposed to be independent of the executive and the legislative branches, although judicial rulings are suspected of being based on political considerations rather than on existing law.[[160]](#cite_note-170)

Military and bureaucratic aristocrats fully controlled political parties between 1946 and the 1980s.[[161]](#cite_note-171): 16 Most parties in Thailand are short-lived.[[162]](#cite_note-Croissant_2010-172): 246 Between 1992 and 2006, Thailand had a [two-party system](/wiki/Two-party_system "Two-party system").[[162]](#cite_note-Croissant_2010-172): 245 Later constitutions created a multi-party system where a single party cannot gain a majority in the house.

A [hereditary monarch](/wiki/Hereditary_monarchy "Hereditary monarchy") serves as Thailand's [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state"). The current King of Thailand is [Vajiralongkorn](/wiki/Vajiralongkorn "Vajiralongkorn") (Rama X), who has reigned since October 2016. The powers of the king are limited by the constitution and he is primarily a symbolic figurehead. However, the monarch still occasionally intervenes in Thai politics, as all constitutions pave the way for customary royal rulings. Some academics outside Thailand, including [Duncan McCargo](/wiki/Duncan_McCargo "Duncan McCargo") and Federico Ferrara, noted extraconstitutional role of the monarch through a "[network monarchy](/wiki/Network_monarchy "Network monarchy")" behind the political scenes.[[163]](#cite_note-173) The monarchy is protected by the severe [*lèse majesté* law](/wiki/L%C3%A8se_majest%C3%A9_in_Thailand "Lèse majesté in Thailand"), even though the people's attitude towards the institution varies from one reign to another.[[164]](#cite_note-174)[[165]](#cite_note-175)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Timeline_of_Prime_Ministers.jpg/250px-Timeline_of_Prime_Ministers.jpg)](/wiki/File:Timeline_of_Prime_Ministers.jpg)

Timeline of Prime Ministers

The kings are protected by *lèse-majesté* laws which allow critics to be jailed for three to fifteen years.[[166]](#cite_note-176) After the [coup d'état in 2014](/wiki/2014_Thai_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "2014 Thai coup d'état"), Thailand had the highest [number of *lèse-majesté* prisoners](/wiki/Category:People_accused_of_l%C3%A8se_majest%C3%A9_in_Thailand "Category:People accused of lèse majesté in Thailand") in the nation's history.[[167]](#cite_note-177)[[168]](#cite_note-178) [Human rights in Thailand](/wiki/Human_rights_in_Thailand "Human rights in Thailand") has been rated *[not free](/wiki/Freedom_in_the_World_(report) "Freedom in the World (report)")* on the [Freedom House](/wiki/Freedom_House "Freedom House") Index since 2014.[[169]](#cite_note-179) On August 7, 2024, Thailand's [Constitutional Court](/wiki/Constitutional_Court_of_Thailand "Constitutional Court of Thailand") banned the victors of the [2023 parliamentary elections](/wiki/2023_Thai_general_election "2023 Thai general election"), the [Move Forward Party](/wiki/Move_Forward_Party "Move Forward Party") and all of its leaders from politics for its proposal to reform the lèse-majesté law, arguing it posed a threat to the constitutional order.[[170]](#cite_note-Economist1-180) *[The Economist](/wiki/The_Economist "The Economist")* criticised the move as an example of "[lawfare](/wiki/Lawfare "Lawfare")" and pointed to the dissolution of its predecessor party, [Future Forward](/wiki/Future_Forward_Party "Future Forward Party") in 2020, as the latest example of how an "alliance of conservative forces in Thailand—including monarchists, the army and a handful of business tycoons—has sought to suppress opposition".[[170]](#cite_note-Economist1-180)[[171]](#cite_note-181)

On the [Freedom in the World 2024 Report](/wiki/Freedom_House "Freedom House") for Thailand, their status improved from *[not free](/wiki/Freedom_in_the_World "Freedom in the World")* to *[partly free](/wiki/Freedom_in_the_world "Freedom in the world")* due to competitive parliamentary elections and the formation of a new governing coalition by what had been a major opposition party, though unelected senators ensured that the party with the most votes was excluded.[[172]](#cite_note-182)

### Administrative divisions

Main articles: [Organization of the government of Thailand](/wiki/Organization_of_the_government_of_Thailand "Organization of the government of Thailand"), [Provinces of Thailand](/wiki/Provinces_of_Thailand "Provinces of Thailand"), and [Administrative Divisions of Thailand](/wiki/Administrative_Divisions_of_Thailand "Administrative Divisions of Thailand")

See also: [Regions of Thailand](/wiki/Regions_of_Thailand "Regions of Thailand")

Thailand is a [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state"); the administrative services of the executive branch are divided into three levels by *National Government Organisation Act, BE 2534* (1991): central, provincial and local. Thailand is composed of 76 [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Thailand "Provinces of Thailand") (จังหวัด, *changwat*),[[173]](#cite_note-183) which are first-level [administrative divisions](/wiki/Administrative_division "Administrative division"). There are also two specially governed districts: the capital [Bangkok](/wiki/Bangkok "Bangkok") and [Pattaya](/wiki/Pattaya "Pattaya"). Bangkok is at provincial level and thus often counted as a province.[[174]](#cite_note-184) Each province is divided into [districts](/wiki/Districts_of_Thailand "Districts of Thailand") (อำเภอ, *amphoe*) and the districts are further divided into sub-districts (ตำบล, *tambons*). The name of each province's capital city (เมือง, mueang) is the same as that of the province. For example, the capital of [Chiang Mai Province](/wiki/Chiang_Mai_Province "Chiang Mai Province") (*Changwat Chiang Mai*) is *Mueang Chiang Mai* or *Chiang Mai*. All provincial governors and district chiefs, which are administrators of provinces and districts respectively, are appointed by the central government.[[175]](#cite_note-185) Thailand's provinces are sometimes grouped into four to six regions, depending on the source.

### Foreign relations

Main article: [Foreign relations of Thailand](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Thailand "Foreign relations of Thailand")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Rama_IX_of_Thailand_and_Barack_Obama.jpg/250px-Rama_IX_of_Thailand_and_Barack_Obama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rama_IX_of_Thailand_and_Barack_Obama.jpg)

King [Bhumibol Adulyadej](/wiki/Bhumibol_Adulyadej "Bhumibol Adulyadej") in a meeting with U.S. President [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama"), 18 November 2012

Siam's and Thailand's way of conducting foreign relations has long been described as "bamboo bending with the wind", of policies that are "always solidly rooted, but flexible enough to bend whichever way the wind blows in order to survive",[[176]](#cite_note-186) or adaptable and pragmatic. In order to secure independence, it sought to pit one great power against the others so that it would be dominated by none.[[177]](#cite_note-187)

During the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"), Thailand sought to prevent the spread of communism so it joined the United States, including participating in [SEATO](/wiki/SEATO "SEATO") alliance, sending expeditions to Korea and Vietnam, and offering the US to use its base. Thailand is one of the five founding members of [Association of Southeast Asian Nations](/wiki/Association_of_Southeast_Asian_Nations "Association of Southeast Asian Nations") (ASEAN), initially to safeguard against communism. The end of [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War") was a turning point in Thai foreign policy and afterwards it sought to improve relations with Communist China and its now-Communist neighbours. Thailand remains an active member of ASEAN and seek to project its influence in it. Thailand has developed increasingly close ties with other members, with progressing regional co-operation in economic, trade, banking, political, and cultural matters.[[178]](#cite_note-188)

In the 2000s, Thailand had taken an active role on the international stage and participated fully in international and regional organisations. It is a [major non-NATO ally](/wiki/Major_non-NATO_ally "Major non-NATO ally") and Priority Watch List [Special 301 Report](/wiki/Special_301_Report "Special 301 Report") of the United States. When [East Timor](/wiki/East_Timor "East Timor") gained independence from Indonesia, Thailand contributed troops to the international peacekeeping effort.[[179]](#cite_note-189) As part of its effort to increase international ties, Thailand had reached out to such regional organisations as the [Organization of American States](/wiki/Organization_of_American_States "Organization of American States") (OAS)[[180]](#cite_note-190) and the [Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe](/wiki/Organization_for_Security_and_Co-operation_in_Europe "Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe") (OSCE).[[181]](#cite_note-191)

During [Thaksin Shinawatra](/wiki/Thaksin_Shinawatra "Thaksin Shinawatra")'s premiership, negotiations for several [free trade agreements](/wiki/Free_trade_agreement "Free trade agreement") with China, Australia, Bahrain, India, and the US were initiated. Thaksin sought to position Thailand as a regional leader, initiating various development projects in poorer neighbouring countries. More controversially, he established close, friendly ties with the Burmese dictatorship.[[182]](#cite_note-192) Thailand joined the US-led [invasion of Iraq](/wiki/2003_invasion_of_Iraq "2003 invasion of Iraq"), sending a [humanitarian contingent](/wiki/Thai_Humanitarian_Assistance_Task_Force_976_Thai-Iraq "Thai Humanitarian Assistance Task Force 976 Thai-Iraq") until September 2004.[[183]](#cite_note-193) Thailand also had contributed troops to reconstruction efforts in [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan").[[184]](#cite_note-194)

In April 2009, the [2008–2013 Cambodian–Thai border crisis](/wiki/2008%E2%80%932013_Cambodian%E2%80%93Thai_border_crisis "2008–2013 Cambodian–Thai border crisis") brought troops on territory immediately adjacent to the 900-year-old ruins of Cambodia's [Preah Vihear](/wiki/Preah_Vihear "Preah Vihear") Hindu temple near the border.[[185]](#cite_note-195)[[186]](#cite_note-196)

After the 2014 coup, Thailand leaned more towards China.[[187]](#cite_note-197) Growing Chinese influence and capital inflow caused some members of parliament to raise the concern about "economic colony" under China after many concessions.[[188]](#cite_note-198)

During the [Israel-Gaza war](/wiki/Gaza_war "Gaza war") in 2023, at first Thailand's prime minister stated that his government strongly condemns the attack against Israel and extends its deepest condolences to the government and the people of Israel[[189]](#cite_note-199) but the government later changed its position and announced that Thailand adopted a neutral stance in this conflict.[[190]](#cite_note-200) 28 Thai nationals were killed in this conflict.[[191]](#cite_note-201)

### Armed forces

Main article: [Royal Thai Armed Forces](/wiki/Royal_Thai_Armed_Forces "Royal Thai Armed Forces")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Chakri_Naruebet_2001.JPEG/250px-Chakri_Naruebet_2001.JPEG)](/wiki/File:Chakri_Naruebet_2001.JPEG)

The [HTMS *Chakri Naruebet*](/wiki/HTMS_Chakri_Naruebet "HTMS Chakri Naruebet"), an [aircraft carrier](/wiki/Aircraft_carrier "Aircraft carrier") of the [Royal Thai Navy](/wiki/Royal_Thai_Navy "Royal Thai Navy")

The [Royal Thai Armed Forces](/wiki/Royal_Thai_Armed_Forces "Royal Thai Armed Forces") (กองทัพไทย; [RTGS](/wiki/Royal_Thai_General_System_of_Transcription "Royal Thai General System of Transcription"): *Kong Thap Thai*) constitute the military of the Kingdom of Thailand. It consists of the [Royal Thai Army](/wiki/Royal_Thai_Army "Royal Thai Army") (กองทัพบกไทย), the [Royal Thai Navy](/wiki/Royal_Thai_Navy "Royal Thai Navy") (กองทัพเรือไทย), and the [Royal Thai Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Thai_Air_Force "Royal Thai Air Force") (กองทัพอากาศไทย). It also incorporates various [paramilitary](/wiki/Paramilitary "Paramilitary") forces.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [head of the Thai Armed Forces](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Thailand "Monarchy of Thailand") (จอมทัพไทย, *Chom Thap Thai*) is the king,[[192]](#cite_note-202) although this position is only nominal. The armed forces are managed by the [Ministry of Defence of Thailand](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defence_(Thailand) "Ministry of Defence (Thailand)"), which is headed by the Minister of Defence (a member of the [cabinet of Thailand](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Thailand "Cabinet of Thailand")) and commanded by the [Royal Thai Armed Forces Headquarters](/wiki/Royal_Thai_Armed_Forces_Headquarters "Royal Thai Armed Forces Headquarters"), which in turn is headed by the [Chief of Defence Forces of Thailand](/wiki/List_of_Commanders_of_the_Royal_Thai_Armed_Forces_Headquarters "List of Commanders of the Royal Thai Armed Forces Headquarters").[[193]](#cite_note-203) Thai annual defence budget almost tripled from US$1.98 billion in 2005 to US$5.88 billion in 2016, accounting for approximately 1.4% of GDP.[[194]](#cite_note-World_Bank_Open_Data-204) Thailand ranked 16th worldwide in the Military Strength Index based on the [Credit Suisse](/wiki/Credit_Suisse "Credit Suisse") report in September 2015.[[195]](#cite_note-CreditSuisse2015-205)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Exercise_Cope_Tiger_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Exercise_Cope_Tiger_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Exercise_Cope_Tiger_(cropped).jpg)

A Royal Thai Air Force [JAS 39 Gripen](/wiki/JAS_39_Gripen "JAS 39 Gripen")

The military is also tasked with humanitarian missions, such as escorting [Rohingya](/wiki/Rohingya "Rohingya") to Malaysia or Indonesia,[[196]](#cite_note-206) ensuring security and welfare for refugees during [Indochina refugee crisis](/wiki/Indochina_refugee_crisis "Indochina refugee crisis").[[197]](#cite_note-207)

According to the constitution, serving in the armed forces is a duty of all Thai citizens.[[198]](#cite_note-208) Thailand still use active draft system for males over the age of 21. They are subjected to varying lengths of active service depending on the duration of reserve training as [Territorial Defence Student](/wiki/Territorial_Defence_Student "Territorial Defence Student") and their level of education. Those who have completed three years or more of reserve training will be exempted entirely. The practice has long been criticised, as some media question its efficacy and value.[[199]](#cite_note-209)[[200]](#cite_note-210) It is alleged that conscripts end up as servants to senior officers[[201]](#cite_note-211) or clerks in military cooperative shops.[[202]](#cite_note-212)[[203]](#cite_note-213) In a report issued in March 2020, [Amnesty International](/wiki/Amnesty_International "Amnesty International") charged that Thai military conscripts face institutionalised abuse systematically hushed up by military authorities.[[204]](#cite_note-214)

Critics observed that Thai military's main objective is to deal with internal rather than external threats.[[205]](#cite_note-Asia_Sentinel_2017-215) [Internal Security Operations Command](/wiki/Internal_Security_Operations_Command "Internal Security Operations Command") is called the political arm of the Thai military, which has overlapping social and political functions with civilian bureaucracy. It also has anti-democracy mission.[[205]](#cite_note-Asia_Sentinel_2017-215) The military is also notorious for numerous corruption incidents, such as accusation of [human trafficking](/wiki/Human_trafficking_in_Thailand "Human trafficking in Thailand"),[[206]](#cite_note-216) and [nepotism](/wiki/Nepotism "Nepotism") in promotion of high-ranking officers.[[207]](#cite_note-217) The military is deeply entrenched in politics. Most recently, the appointed senators include more than 100 active and retired military.[[208]](#cite_note-218)

Thailand is the 75th most peaceful country in the world, according to the 2024 [Global Peace Index](/wiki/Global_Peace_Index "Global Peace Index").[[209]](#cite_note-219)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Thailand](/wiki/Economy_of_Thailand "Economy of Thailand")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Bangkok_%28251385359%29.jpeg/250px-Bangkok_%28251385359%29.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Bangkok_(251385359).jpeg)

[Sathorn](/wiki/Sathorn "Sathorn") in Bangkok is a skyscraper-studded business district that is home to major hotels and embassies.

The economy of Thailand is heavily export-dependent, with exports accounting for more than two-thirds of [gross domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") (GDP). Thailand exports over US$105 billion worth of goods and services annually.[[1]](#cite_note-CIA-2) Major exports include cars, computers, electrical appliances, [rice](/wiki/Rice_production_in_Thailand "Rice production in Thailand"), textiles and footwear, fishery products, rubber, and jewellery.[[1]](#cite_note-CIA-2)

Thailand is an [emerging economy](/wiki/Emerging_markets "Emerging markets") and is considered a [newly industrialised country](/wiki/Newly_industrialised_country "Newly industrialised country"). Thailand had a 2017 GDP of US$1.236 trillion (on a [purchasing power parity](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity") basis).[[215]](#cite_note-225) Thailand is the second largest economy in Southeast Asia after Indonesia. Thailand ranks midway in the wealth spread in Southeast Asia as it is the fourth richest nation according to GDP per capita, after Singapore, Brunei, and Malaysia.

Thailand functions as an [anchor economy](/wiki/Anchor_economy "Anchor economy") for the neighbouring developing economies of Laos, Myanmar, and Cambodia. In the third quarter of 2014, the unemployment rate in Thailand stood at 0.84% according to Thailand's National Economic and Social Development Board (NESDB).[[216]](#cite_note-226)

In 2017, the Thai economy grew an inflation-adjusted 3.9%, up from 3.3% in 2016, marking its fastest expansion since 2012.[[217]](#cite_note-227) High public spending, especially during the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Thailand "COVID-19 pandemic in Thailand"), prompted the authorities to raise Thailand's public debt ceiling from 60% to 70% of GDP.[[218]](#cite_note-228)

As of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Thailand&action=edit), Thailand struggles with low productivity, poor education, high household debt, low private investment and slow economic growth,[[219]](#cite_note-229)[[220]](#cite_note-230) with an economic research group forecasted an annual GDP growth of below 2% in the next decades without structural reforms.[[221]](#cite_note-231)

As of 2025, The Bank of Thailand's economic outlook page projects Thai GDP growth of around 2.2% in 2025 and 1.6% in 2026, noting that growth in the first half of 2025 was supported by front‑loaded exports to the U.S. ahead of tariff hikes, while the second half is expected to slow as higher U.S. tariffs bite.[[222]](#cite_note-232)

### Income and wealth disparities

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Thailand_GDP_Per_Capita_Development_1890-2022.png/250px-Thailand_GDP_Per_Capita_Development_1890-2022.png)](/wiki/File:Thailand_GDP_Per_Capita_Development_1890-2022.png)

Development of real GDP per capita, 1890 to 2022

Thais have median wealth per one adult person of $1,469 in 2016,[[223]](#cite_note-Wealth_2016-233): 98 increasing from $605 in 2010.[[223]](#cite_note-Wealth_2016-233): 34 In 2016, Thailand was [ranked 87th in Human Development Index](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_Human_Development_Index "List of countries by Human Development Index"), and [70th in the inequality-adjusted HDI](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_inequality-adjusted_HDI "List of countries by inequality-adjusted HDI").[[224]](#cite_note-234)

In 2017, Thailand's median household income was ฿26,946 per month.[[225]](#cite_note-NSO_2560-235): 1 Top quintile households had a 45.0% share of all income, while bottom quintile households had 7.1%.[[225]](#cite_note-NSO_2560-235): 4 There were 26.9 million persons who had the bottom 40% of income earning less than ฿5,344 per person per month.[[226]](#cite_note-NESDB_2559-236): 5 During the [2013–2014 Thai political crisis](/wiki/2013%E2%80%932014_Thai_political_crisis "2013–2014 Thai political crisis"), a survey found that anti-government [PDRC](/wiki/People%27s_Democratic_Reform_Committee "People's Democratic Reform Committee") mostly (32%) had a monthly income of more than ฿50,000, while pro-government [UDD](/wiki/United_Front_for_Democracy_Against_Dictatorship "United Front for Democracy Against Dictatorship") mostly (27%) had between ฿10,000 and ฿20,000.[[227]](#cite_note-237): 7

In 2014, [Credit Suisse](/wiki/Credit_Suisse "Credit Suisse") reported that Thailand was the world's third most unequal country, behind Russia and India.[[228]](#cite_note-BBC_2017-238) The top 10% richest held 79% of the country's assets.[[228]](#cite_note-BBC_2017-238) The top 1% held 58% of the assets.[[228]](#cite_note-BBC_2017-238) The 50 richest Thai families had a total net worth accounting to 30% of GDP.[[228]](#cite_note-BBC_2017-238) [Bank of Thailand](/wiki/Bank_of_Thailand "Bank of Thailand") reported that during 2006–16, Thailand's top 5% largest companies had 85% of all corporate revenue in the nation, and only 6% of the country's companies were in export industries, which made up 60% of the country's GDP.[[229]](#cite_note-239)

In 2016, 5.81 million people lived in poverty, or 11.6 million people (17.2% of population) if "near poor" is included.[[226]](#cite_note-NESDB_2559-236): 1 The proportion of the poor relative to total population in each region was 12.96% in the Northeast, 12.35% in the South, and 9.83% in the North.[[226]](#cite_note-NESDB_2559-236): 2 In 2017, there were 14 million people who applied for social welfare (yearly income of less than ฿100,000 was required).[[228]](#cite_note-BBC_2017-238) In the first quarter of 2023, Thai household debts totalled 14.6 trillion baht or 89.2% of GDP; the average debt per household was approximately 500,000 baht.[[230]](#cite_note-240) In 2016, there were estimated 30,000 homeless persons in the country.[[231]](#cite_note-241)

### Exports and manufacturing

The economy of Thailand is heavily export-dependent, with exports accounting for more than two-thirds of gross domestic products (GDPs). Major exports include cars, computers, electrical appliances, [rice](/wiki/Rice_production_in_Thailand "Rice production in Thailand"), textiles and footwear, fishery products, rubber, and jewellery.[[1]](#cite_note-CIA-2) In 2022, Thailand's export of goods is worth roughly US$290 billion while its import worth roughly US$305 billion.[[232]](#cite_note-242)

Substantial industries include electric appliances, components, computer components, and vehicles. Thailand's recovery from the 1997–1998 Asian financial crisis depended mainly on exports, among various other factors. As of 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Thailand&action=edit), the [Thai automotive industry](/wiki/Automotive_industry_in_Thailand "Automotive industry in Thailand") was the largest in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") and the [9th largest in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_motor_vehicle_production "List of countries by motor vehicle production").[[233]](#cite_note-243)[[234]](#cite_note-244)[[235]](#cite_note-OICA_2013-245) The Thailand industry has an annual output of near 1.5 million vehicles, mostly commercial vehicles.[[235]](#cite_note-OICA_2013-245)

Most of the vehicles built in Thailand are developed and licensed by foreign producers, mainly Japanese and American. The Thai car industry takes advantage of the [ASEAN Free Trade Area](/wiki/ASEAN_Free_Trade_Area "ASEAN Free Trade Area") (AFTA) to find a market for many of its products. Eight manufacturers, five Japanese, two US, and [Tata](/wiki/Tata_Group "Tata Group") of India, produce pick-up trucks in Thailand.[[236]](#cite_note-Takahashi_2014-246) As of 2012, due to its favourable taxation for 2-door pick-ups at only 3–12% against 17–50% for passenger cars, Thailand was the second largest consumer of pick-up trucks in the world, after the US.[[237]](#cite_note-247) In 2014, pick-ups accounted for 42% of all new vehicle sales in Thailand.[[236]](#cite_note-Takahashi_2014-246)

### Tourism

Further information: [Tourism in Thailand](/wiki/Tourism_in_Thailand "Tourism in Thailand")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/0000140_-_Wat_Arun_Ratchawararam_005.jpg/250px-0000140_-_Wat_Arun_Ratchawararam_005.jpg)](/wiki/File:0000140_-_Wat_Arun_Ratchawararam_005.jpg)

[Wat Arun](/wiki/Wat_Arun "Wat Arun"), [Bangkok](/wiki/Bangkok "Bangkok"), is among the best-known Thailand's landmarks.

Tourism makes up about 6% of the country's economy. Prior to the pandemic, Thailand was the world's eighth most visited country according to the [World Tourism rankings](/wiki/World_Tourism_rankings "World Tourism rankings") compiled by the [United Nations World Tourism Organisation](/wiki/United_Nations_World_Tourism_Organization "United Nations World Tourism Organization"). In 2019, Thailand received 39.8 million international tourists, ahead of [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") and [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany")[[238]](#cite_note-248) and was the fourth highest in international tourism earning 60.5 billion [US dollars](/wiki/US_dollars "US dollars").

Thailand was the most visited country in Southeast Asia in 2013, according to the World Tourism Organisation. Estimates of tourism receipts directly contributing to the Thai GDP of 12 trillion baht range from 9 per cent (1 trillion baht) (2013) to 16 per cent.[[239]](#cite_note-249) When including the indirect effects of tourism, it is said to account for 20.2 per cent (2.4 trillion baht) of Thailand's GDP.[[240]](#cite_note-250): 1

Asian tourists primarily visit Thailand for Bangkok and the historical, natural, and cultural sights in its vicinity. Western tourists not only visit Bangkok and surrounding areas; many travel to the southern beaches and islands. The north is the chief destination for trekking and [adventure travel](/wiki/Adventure_travel "Adventure travel") with its diverse ethnic minority groups and forested mountains. The region hosting the fewest tourists is [Isan](/wiki/Isan "Isan"). To accommodate foreign visitors, a separate tourism police with offices were set up in the major tourist areas and an emergency telephone number.[[241]](#cite_note-251)

Thailand ranks as the worlds fifth largest [medical tourism](/wiki/Medical_tourism "Medical tourism") destination in spending, according to the [World Travel and Tourism Council](/wiki/World_Travel_and_Tourism_Council "World Travel and Tourism Council"), attracting over 2.5 million visitors in 2018,[[242]](#cite_note-252) and is number one in Asia.[[243]](#cite_note-253) The country is popular for the growing practice of [sex reassignment surgery](/wiki/Sex_reassignment_surgery "Sex reassignment surgery") (SRS) and cosmetic surgery. In 2010–2012, more than 90% of medical tourists travelled to Thailand for SRS.[[244]](#cite_note-254) [Prostitution in Thailand](/wiki/Prostitution_in_Thailand "Prostitution in Thailand") and sex tourism also form a *de facto* part of the economy. Campaigns promote Thailand as exotic to attract tourists.[[245]](#cite_note-255) One estimate published in 2003 placed the trade at US$4.3 billion per year or about 3% of the Thai economy.[[246]](#cite_note-256) It is believed that at least 10% of tourist dollars are spent on the sex trade.[[247]](#cite_note-257)

### Agriculture and natural resources

Further information: [Agriculture in Thailand](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Thailand "Agriculture in Thailand")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Rice_farmers_Mae_Wang_Chiang_Mai_Province.jpg/500px-Rice_farmers_Mae_Wang_Chiang_Mai_Province.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rice_farmers_Mae_Wang_Chiang_Mai_Province.jpg)

Thailand has long been [one of the largest rice exporters in the world](/wiki/Rice_production_in_Thailand "Rice production in Thailand"). Forty-nine per cent of Thailand's labour force is employed in agriculture.[[248]](#cite_note-Leturque_2010-258)

Forty-nine per cent of Thailand's labour force is employed in [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Thailand "Agriculture in Thailand").[[248]](#cite_note-Leturque_2010-258) This is down from 70% in 1980.[[248]](#cite_note-Leturque_2010-258) Rice is the most important crop in the country and Thailand had long been the world's leading exporter of rice, until recently falling behind both India and Vietnam.[[249]](#cite_note-259) Thailand has the highest percentage of arable land, 27.25%, of any state in the [Greater Mekong Subregion](/wiki/Greater_Mekong_Subregion "Greater Mekong Subregion").[[250]](#cite_note-260) About 55% of the arable land area is used for rice production.[[251]](#cite_note-261)

Agriculture has been experiencing a transition from labour-intensive and transitional methods to a more industrialised and competitive sector.[[248]](#cite_note-Leturque_2010-258) Between 1962 and 1983, the agricultural sector grew by 4.1% per year on average and continued to grow at 2.2% between 1983 and 2007.[[248]](#cite_note-Leturque_2010-258) The relative contribution of agriculture to GDP has declined while exports of goods and services have increased.

Furthermore, access to [biocapacity](/wiki/Biocapacity "Biocapacity") in Thailand is lower than world average. In 2016, Thailand had 1.2 global hectares[[252]](#cite_note-Global_Footprint_Network-262) of biocapacity per person within its territory, a little less than world average of 1.6 global hectares per person.[[253]](#cite_note-263) In contrast, in 2016, they used 2.5 global hectares of biocapacity—their [ecological footprint](/wiki/Ecological_footprint "Ecological footprint") of consumption. This means they use about twice as much biocapacity as Thailand contains, resulting in a deficit.[[252]](#cite_note-Global_Footprint_Network-262)

### Informal economy

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Train_Night_Market_Ratchada_%2834079339800%29.jpg/250px-Train_Night_Market_Ratchada_%2834079339800%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Train_Night_Market_Ratchada_(34079339800).jpg)

[The Train Night Market](/wiki/Talat_Rotfai "Talat Rotfai") in Bangkok

In 2012, it was estimated that informal workers comprised 62.6% of the Thai workforce. The [Ministry of Labour](/wiki/Ministry_of_Labour_(Thailand) "Ministry of Labour (Thailand)") defines informal workers to be individuals who work in informal economies and do not have employee status under a given country's Labour Protection Act (LPA). The informal sector in Thailand has grown significantly over the past 60 years over the course of Thailand's gradual transition from an agriculture-based economy to becoming more industrialised and service-oriented.[[254]](#cite_note-Kongtip-2015-264) Between 1993 and 1995, ten per cent of the Thai labour force moved from the agricultural sector to urban and industrial jobs, especially in the manufacturing sector. It is estimated that between 1988 and 1995, the number of factory workers in the country doubled from two to four million, as Thailand's GDP tripled.[[255]](#cite_note-265)

While the [Asian financial crisis](/wiki/1997_Asian_financial_crisis "1997 Asian financial crisis") that followed in 1997 hit the Thai economy hard, the industrial sector continued to expand under widespread deregulation, as Thailand was mandated to adopt a range of structural adjustment reforms upon receiving funding from the IMF and World Bank. These reforms implemented an agenda of increased privatisation and trade liberalisation in the country, and decreased federal subsidisation of public goods and utilities, agricultural price supports, and regulations on fair wages and labour conditions.[[256]](#cite_note-Guille_2014-266)[*[full citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#What_information_to_include "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Many migrant farmers took jobs in sweatshops and factories with few labour regulations and often exploitative conditions.[[257]](#cite_note-267) Those that could not find formal factory work, including illegal migrants and the families of rural Thai migrants, are under the regulation imposed by the structural adjustment programmes. Scholars argue that the economic consequences and social costs of Thailand's labour reforms in the wake of the 1997 Asian financial crisis fell on individuals and families rather than the state.[[256]](#cite_note-Guille_2014-266)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Songkran_Day_%40_Chiangmai_Wall.jpg/250px-Songkran_Day_%40_Chiangmai_Wall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Songkran_Day_@_Chiangmai_Wall.jpg)

[Songkran](/wiki/Songkran_(Thailand) "Songkran (Thailand)") Festival, [Chiang Mai](/wiki/Chiang_Mai "Chiang Mai")'s ancient city wall

Informal labour in entertainment, nightlife, and the sex industry face additional vulnerabilities, including recruitment into circles of sexual exploitation and human trafficking.[[254]](#cite_note-Kongtip-2015-264) A 2012 study found that 64% of informal workers had not completed education beyond primary school. Many informal workers are also migrants, only some of which have legal status in the country. The informal labour sector is also not recognised under the Labour Protection Act (LPA). Thai social security policies fail to protect against workplace accidents and unemployment and retirement insurance. Many informal workers are not legally contracted for their employment, and many do not make a living wage.[[254]](#cite_note-Kongtip-2015-264) Tens of thousands of migrants from neighbouring countries face exploitation in a few industries,[[258]](#cite_note-268) especially in fishing where slave-like conditions have been reported.[[259]](#cite_note-269)

### Science and technology

Thailand ranked 45th in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025.[[260]](#cite_note-270)[[261]](#cite_note-271) The [Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation](/wiki/Ministry_of_Higher_Education,_Science,_Research_and_Innovation "Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation") and [its agencies](/wiki/Ministry_of_Higher_Education,_Science,_Research_and_Innovation#Organization "Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation") oversees the development of science, technology, and research in Thailand. According to the National Research Council of Thailand, the country devoted 1.1% of its GDP to the research and development of science in 2019, with over 166,788 research and development personnel in full-time equivalent that year.[[262]](#cite_note-272)[[263]](#cite_note-273)

## Infrastructure

### Transportation

Main articles: [Transport in Thailand](/wiki/Transport_in_Thailand "Transport in Thailand") and [List of airports in Thailand](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_Thailand "List of airports in Thailand")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Bangkok_Skytrain_2011.jpg/250px-Bangkok_Skytrain_2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bangkok_Skytrain_2011.jpg)

The [BTS Skytrain](/wiki/BTS_Skytrain "BTS Skytrain") is an elevated rapid transit system in Bangkok.

The [State Railway of Thailand](/wiki/State_Railway_of_Thailand "State Railway of Thailand") (SRT) operates all of Thailand's national rail lines. [Krung Thep Aphiwat Central Terminal](/wiki/Krung_Thep_Aphiwat_Central_Terminal "Krung Thep Aphiwat Central Terminal") and [Bangkok (Hua Lamphong)](/wiki/Bangkok_(Hua_Lamphong)_railway_station "Bangkok (Hua Lamphong) railway station") are the main termini of intercity routes. Phahonyothin and ICD [Lat Krabang](/wiki/Lat_Krabang "Lat Krabang") are the main freight terminals. As of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Thailand&action=edit) SRT had 4,507 km (2,801 mi) of track, all of it [metre gauge](/wiki/Meter_gauge "Meter gauge"). Nearly all is single-track (2702.1 km), although some important sections around Bangkok are double (1,234.9 km or 767.3 mi) or triple-tracked (107 km or 66 mi), and there are plans to extend this.[[264]](#cite_note-274)[[265]](#cite_note-275)

[Rail transport in Bangkok](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Bangkok "Rail transport in Bangkok") includes long-distance services. There are four rapid transit rail systems in the capital: the [BTS Skytrain](/wiki/BTS_Skytrain "BTS Skytrain"), [MRT](/wiki/MRT_(Bangkok) "MRT (Bangkok)"), [SRT Red Lines](/wiki/SRT_Red_Lines "SRT Red Lines"), and the [Airport Rail Link](/wiki/Airport_Rail_Link_(Bangkok) "Airport Rail Link (Bangkok)").[[266]](#cite_note-276) In Bangkok, there were two failed rapid rail projects [Lavalin Skytrain](/wiki/Lavalin_Skytrain "Lavalin Skytrain") and [Bangkok Elevated Road and Train System](/wiki/Bangkok_Elevated_Road_and_Train_System "Bangkok Elevated Road and Train System"), before [Mass Rapid Transit Master Plan in Bangkok Metropolitan Region](/wiki/Mass_Rapid_Transit_Master_Plan_in_Bangkok_Metropolitan_Region "Mass Rapid Transit Master Plan in Bangkok Metropolitan Region") was endorsed by the cabinet on 27 September 1994 and implemented from 1995 to the present.[[267]](#cite_note-adjustplan-277)

Thailand has 390,000 kilometres (240,000 miles) of highways.[[268]](#cite_note-278) As of 2017[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Thailand&action=edit), Thailand has over 462,133 roads and 37 million registered vehicles, 20 million of them motorbikes.[[269]](#cite_note-279) A number of undivided two-lane highways have been converted into divided four-lane highways. Within the Bangkok Metropolitan Region, there are a number of [controlled-access highways](/wiki/Controlled-access_highways_in_Thailand "Controlled-access highways in Thailand"). There are 4,125 public vans operating on 114 routes from Bangkok alone.[[270]](#cite_note-280) Other forms of road transport includes [tuk-tuks](/wiki/Auto_rickshaw "Auto rickshaw"), taxis—with over 80,647 registered taxis nationwide as of 2018,[[271]](#cite_note-281) vans ([minibus](/wiki/Minibus "Minibus")), motorbike taxis, and [songthaews](/wiki/Songthaew "Songthaew").

As of 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Thailand&action=edit), Thailand has 103 airports with 63 paved runways, in addition to 6 heliports. The busiest airport in the country is Bangkok's [Suvarnabhumi Airport](/wiki/Suvarnabhumi_Airport "Suvarnabhumi Airport").[[272]](#cite_note-282)

### Energy

Further information: [Energy in Thailand](/wiki/Energy_in_Thailand "Energy in Thailand")

75% of Thailand's electrical generation is powered by [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas") in 2014.[[273]](#cite_note-Energy_2013-283) Coal-fired power plants produce an additional 20% of electricity, with the remainder coming from biomass, hydro, and biogas.[[273]](#cite_note-Energy_2013-283) Compared to other ASEAN's countries, Thailand is the largest importer of gas in weight.[[274]](#cite_note-ASEAN_Centre_for_Energy-2023-284): 17 In 2022, Thailand's oil and gas production dropped by 19% and 17%, respectively.[[275]](#cite_note-285)[[274]](#cite_note-ASEAN_Centre_for_Energy-2023-284): 8

The government, in 2018, has developed an Alternative Energy Development Plan 2018–2037 (AEDP 2018). The plan defines goals for the increase of renewable energy to almost 30,000 MW by 2037.[[276]](#cite_note-286)[[277]](#cite_note-287)

## Demographics

Main article: [Demographics of Thailand](/wiki/Demographics_of_Thailand "Demographics of Thailand")

Ethnic groups
of Thailand  
(2015 estimate by [The World Factbook](/wiki/The_World_Factbook "The World Factbook"))[[1]](#cite_note-CIA-2)

1. [Thai](/wiki/Thai_people "Thai people") (97.5%)
2. [Burmese](/wiki/Burmese_in_Thailand "Burmese in Thailand") (1.30%)
3. other (1.10%)
4. unspecified (0.10%)

Thailand had a population of 65.95 million on 8 Jan 2025.[[278]](#cite_note-Khaosod_English-2025-288) There were 64,953,661 Thai nationals and 997,549 non-Thai residents.[[278]](#cite_note-Khaosod_English-2025-288) Thailand's first census in 1909 found the population to be 8.2 million.[[279]](#cite_note-289) Thailand's population is largely rural, concentrated in the rice-growing areas of the central, northeastern, and northern regions. About 44.2% of Thailand's population lived in urban areas as of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Thailand&action=edit), slowly increasing from 29.4% in the 1990 census and 31.1% in the 2000 census.[[280]](#cite_note-290)

Thailand's government-sponsored family planning programme resulted in a dramatic decline in population growth from 3.1% in 1960 to around 0.4% today. In 1970, an average of 5.7 people lived in a Thai household; in 2022, the average Thai household size was 3 people.[[281]](#cite_note-291) Now, more than 20% of its population is aged over 60 and has a low birth rate, posing economic challenges.[[282]](#cite_note-292) The [sex ratio](/wiki/Sex_ratio "Sex ratio") between male and female is 1.05, with Thailand having slightly more males.[[283]](#cite_note-293) Thailand's [total fertility rate](/wiki/Total_fertility_rate "Total fertility rate") declined to 1.0 in 2024.[[284]](#cite_note-chairith-294)[[278]](#cite_note-Khaosod_English-2025-288) This ranks Thailand among countries with [ultra-low fertility](/wiki/Sub-replacement_fertility "Sub-replacement fertility") rates.[[284]](#cite_note-chairith-294)[[278]](#cite_note-Khaosod_English-2025-288)

### Ethnic groups

Further information: [Ethnic groups in Thailand](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Thailand "Ethnic groups in Thailand")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Thailand_%28272021437%29.jpg/250px-Thailand_%28272021437%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thailand_(272021437).jpg)

Hill tribes girls in the Northeast of Thailand

As of 2010, Thai people make up the majority of Thailand's population (95.9%). The remaining 4.1% of the population are Burmese (2.0%), others (1.3%), and unspecified (0.9%).[[1]](#cite_note-CIA-2)

According to genetic research, the present-day Thai people were divided into three groups: the northern group (Khon Mueang) are closely related to the [Tai](/wiki/Tai_people "Tai people") ethnic groups in [southern China](/wiki/Southern_China "Southern China"), the northeastern group ([Isan people](/wiki/Isan_people "Isan people")) are mixed Tai and several [Austroasiatic](/wiki/Austroasiatic "Austroasiatic")-speaking ethnic groups, while the central and southern groups (formerly called Siamese) strongly share genetic profiles with the [Mon people](/wiki/Mon_people "Mon people").[[37]](#cite_note-genetic-45)[[38]](#cite_note-genetic2-46)[[39]](#cite_note-genetic3-47)

According to the Royal Thai Government's 2011 Country Report to the UN Committee responsible for the [International Convention for the Elimination of All Forms of Racial Discrimination](/wiki/International_Convention_on_the_Elimination_of_All_Forms_of_Racial_Discrimination "International Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Racial Discrimination"), available from the Department of Rights and Liberties Promotion of the Thai Ministry of Justice,: 3 62 ethnic communities are officially recognised in Thailand. Twenty million Central Thai (together with approximately 650,000 [Khorat Thai](/wiki/Khorat_Thai "Khorat Thai")) made up approximately 20,650,000 (34.1 per cent) of the state's population of 60,544,937[[285]](#cite_note-295) at the time of completion of the Mahidol University *Ethnolinguistic Maps of Thailand* data (1997).[[286]](#cite_note-Ethnolinguistic_2004-296)

The 2011 Thailand Country Report provides population numbers for mountain peoples ('hill tribes') and ethnic communities in the Northeast and is explicit about its main reliance on the Mahidol University Ethnolinguistic Maps of Thailand data.[[286]](#cite_note-Ethnolinguistic_2004-296) Thus, though over 3.288 million people in the Northeast alone could not be categorised, the population and percentages of other ethnic communities c. 1997 are known for all of Thailand and constitute minimum populations. In descending order, the largest (equal to or greater than 400,000) are a) 15,080,000 Lao (24.9 per cent) consisting of the Thai Lao (14 million) and other smaller Lao groups, namely the Thai Loei (400–500,000), Lao Lom (350,000), Lao Wiang/Klang (200,000), Lao Khrang (90,000), Lao Ngaew (30,000), and Lao Ti (10,000); b) six million Khon Muang (9.9 per cent, also called Northern Thais); c) 4.5 million Pak Tai (7.5 per cent, also called Southern Thais); d) 1.4 million Khmer Leu (2.3 per cent, also called Northern Khmer); e) 900,000 Malay (1.5%); f) 500,000 Nyaw (0.8 per cent); g) 470,000 Phu Thai (0.8 per cent); h) 400,000 Kuy/Kuay (also known as Suay) (0.7 per cent), and i) 350,000 Karen (0.6 per cent).: 7–13 [Thai Chinese](/wiki/Thai_Chinese "Thai Chinese"), including those of significant Chinese origins, are 14% of the population, while Thais with partial Chinese ancestry comprise up to 40% of the population.[[287]](#cite_note-297) [Thai Malays](/wiki/Thai_Malays "Thai Malays") also represent 3% of the Thai population's heritage, with the remainder consisting of [Mons](/wiki/Mon_people "Mon people"), [Khmers](/wiki/Khmers "Khmers"), and various "[hill tribes](/wiki/Hill_tribe_(Thailand) "Hill tribe (Thailand)")".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Increasing numbers of migrants from neighbouring Myanmar, Laos, and Cambodia, as well as from Nepal and India, have pushed the total number of non-national residents to around 3.5 million as of 2009[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Thailand&action=edit), up from an estimated 2 million in 2008.[[288]](#cite_note-298) Some 41,000 [Britons](/wiki/British_people "British people") and 20,000 [Australians](/wiki/Australians "Australians") live in Thailand.[[289]](#cite_note-299)[[290]](#cite_note-300)
