### Language

Main article: [Languages of Thailand](/wiki/Languages_of_Thailand "Languages of Thailand")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Thailand_ethnic_map.svg/250px-Thailand_ethnic_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Thailand_ethnic_map.svg)

An ethnolinguistic map of Thailand

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Bangkok_National_Museum_-_2017-04-22_%28008%29.jpg/250px-Bangkok_National_Museum_-_2017-04-22_%28008%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bangkok_National_Museum_-_2017-04-22_(008).jpg)

The Silajaruek of [Sukhothai Kingdom](/wiki/Sukhothai_Kingdom "Sukhothai Kingdom") are hundreds of stone inscriptions that form a historical record of the period.

[Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language") is the official language. It is a [Kra–Dai language](/wiki/Kra%E2%80%93Dai_language "Kra–Dai language") closely related to [Lao](/wiki/Lao_language "Lao language"), [Shan](/wiki/Shan_language "Shan language") in Myanmar, and numerous smaller languages spoken in an arc from [Hainan](/wiki/Hainan "Hainan") and [Yunnan](/wiki/Yunnan "Yunnan") south to the Chinese border. It is the principal language of education and government and spoken throughout the country.[[291]](#cite_note-Lewis-2009-301) The standard is based on the dialect of the central Thai people, and it is written in the [Thai alphabet](/wiki/Thai_alphabet "Thai alphabet"), an [abugida](/wiki/Abugida "Abugida") script that evolved from the [Khmer alphabet](/wiki/Khmer_alphabet "Khmer alphabet").[[292]](#cite_note-hartmann-302)

Sixty-two languages were recognised by the Royal Thai Government.[[293]](#cite_note-303) For the purposes of the national census, four dialects of Thai exist; these partly coincide with regional designations, such as [Southern Thai](/wiki/Southern_Thai_language "Southern Thai language") and [Northern Thai](/wiki/Northern_Thai_language "Northern Thai language").[[291]](#cite_note-Lewis-2009-301)

The largest of Thailand's minority languages is the [Lao](/wiki/Lao_language "Lao language") dialect of [Isan](/wiki/Isan_language "Isan language") spoken in the northeastern provinces. In the far south, [Kelantan-Pattani Malay](/wiki/Kelantan-Pattani_Malay "Kelantan-Pattani Malay") is the primary language of Malay Muslims. Varieties of Chinese are also spoken by the large [Thai Chinese](/wiki/Thai_Chinese "Thai Chinese") population, with the [Teochew dialect](/wiki/Teochew_dialect "Teochew dialect") best-represented. Numerous tribal languages are also spoken, including many [Austroasiatic languages](/wiki/Austroasiatic_languages "Austroasiatic languages") such as [Mon](/wiki/Mon_language "Mon language"), [Khmer](/wiki/Khmer_language "Khmer language"), [Lawa](/wiki/Lawa_language "Lawa language") and [Mlabri](/wiki/Mlabri_language "Mlabri language"); [Austronesian languages](/wiki/Austronesian_languages "Austronesian languages") such as [Cham](/wiki/Cham_language "Cham language"), [Moken](/wiki/Moken_language "Moken language") and [Urak Lawoi'](/wiki/Urak_Lawoi%27_language "Urak Lawoi' language"); [Sino-Tibetan languages](/wiki/Sino-Tibetan_languages "Sino-Tibetan languages") like [Akha](/wiki/Akha_language "Akha language") and [Karen](/wiki/Karen_languages "Karen languages"); and other [Tai languages](/wiki/Tai_languages "Tai languages") such as [Phu Thai](/wiki/Phu_Thai_language "Phu Thai language"), and [Saek](/wiki/Saek_language "Saek language"). [Hmong](/wiki/Hmong_language "Hmong language") is a member of the [Hmong–Mien languages](/wiki/Hmong%E2%80%93Mien_languages "Hmong–Mien languages"), which is now regarded as a language family of its own.[[294]](#cite_note-304)[[291]](#cite_note-Lewis-2009-301)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Thailand](/wiki/Religion_in_Thailand "Religion in Thailand")

Religion in Thailand (2018)[[2]](#cite_note-NSO-3)

1. [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Thailand "Buddhism in Thailand") (93.5%)
2. [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_Thailand "Islam in Thailand") (5.37%)
3. [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity_in_Thailand "Christianity in Thailand") (1.13%)
4. [Other](/wiki/Religion_in_Thailand "Religion in Thailand") (0.04%)

The country's most prevalent religion is [Theravada](/wiki/Theravada "Theravada") Buddhism, which is an integral part of Thai identity and culture. Active participation in Buddhism is among the highest in the world. Thailand has the second-largest number of [Buddhists](/wiki/Buddhist "Buddhist") in the world after China.[[295]](#cite_note-305) According to the 2018 [National Statistical Office](/wiki/National_Statistical_Office_(Thailand) "National Statistical Office (Thailand)") data, 93.46% of the country's population self-identified as Buddhists.[[2]](#cite_note-NSO-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Phutthamonthon_Buddha.JPG/250px-Phutthamonthon_Buddha.JPG)](/wiki/File:Phutthamonthon_Buddha.JPG)

*[Samanera](/wiki/Samanera "Samanera")* of [Theravada](/wiki/Theravada "Theravada") Buddhism, the most practised religion in Thailand

[Muslims](/wiki/Islam_in_Thailand "Islam in Thailand") constitute the second largest religious group in Thailand, comprising 5.37% of the population in 2018.[[2]](#cite_note-NSO-3) Islam is concentrated mostly in the country's southernmost provinces: [Pattani](/wiki/Pattani_Province "Pattani Province"), [Yala](/wiki/Yala_Province "Yala Province"), [Satun](/wiki/Satun_Province "Satun Province"), [Narathiwat](/wiki/Narathiwat_Province "Narathiwat Province"), and part of [Songkhla](/wiki/Songkhla_Province "Songkhla Province"), which are predominantly [Malay](/wiki/Malays_(ethnic_group) "Malays (ethnic group)"), most of whom are [Sunni Muslims](/wiki/Sunni_Muslim "Sunni Muslim"). Christians represented 1.13% of the population in 2018, with the remaining population consisting of [Hindus](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Thailand "Hinduism in Thailand") and [Sikhs](/wiki/Sikhism_in_Thailand "Sikhism in Thailand"), who live mostly in the country's cities. There is also a small [Jewish community in Thailand](/wiki/Jews_and_Judaism_in_Thailand "Jews and Judaism in Thailand") dating back to the 17th century.[[296]](#cite_note-306)

The constitution does not name an official state religion, and provides for freedom of religion. There have been no widespread reports of societal abuses or discrimination based on religious belief or practice.[[297]](#cite_note-307) Thai law officially recognises five religious groups: Buddhists, Muslims, Brahmin-Hindus, Sikhs, and Christians.[[298]](#cite_note-308) However, some laws are inspired from Buddhist practices, such as banning alcohol sales on religious holidays.[[299]](#cite_note-309)

### Education

Main article: [Education in Thailand](/wiki/Education_in_Thailand "Education in Thailand")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Chulalongkorn_University_Auditorium.jpg/250px-Chulalongkorn_University_Auditorium.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chulalongkorn_University_Auditorium.jpg)

[Chulalongkorn University](/wiki/Chulalongkorn_University "Chulalongkorn University"), established in 1917, is the oldest university in Thailand.

In 1995, as minister of education, [Sukavich Rangsitpollaid](/wiki/Sukavich_Rangsitpol "Sukavich Rangsitpol") let out his plans for educational reform in Thailand.[[300]](#cite_note-310) The reform was considered a landmark movement after nearly 100 years of education under the previous system.[[301]](#cite_note-311) Thailand's youth literacy rate was 98.1% in 2015.[[302]](#cite_note-312) Education is provided by a school system of kindergartens, primary, lower secondary and upper secondary schools, numerous vocational colleges, and universities. Education is compulsory up to and including age 14, while the government is mandated to provide free education through to age 17. Issues concerning university entrance have been in constant upheaval for a number of years. The country is also one of the few that still mandates uniform up to the university years, which is still a subject of ongoing debate.[[303]](#cite_note-313)

In 2013, the [Ministry of Information and Communication Technology](/wiki/Ministry_of_Information_and_Communication_Technology_(Thailand) "Ministry of Information and Communication Technology (Thailand)") announced that 27,231 schools would receive classroom-level access to [high-speed internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet").[[304]](#cite_note-314) However, the country's educational infrastructure was still underprepared for online teaching, as smaller and more remote schools were particularly hindered by COVID-19 restrictions.[[305]](#cite_note-315)

The number of higher education institutions in Thailand has grown over the past decades to 156 officially. The two top-ranking universities in Thailand are [Chulalongkorn University](/wiki/Chulalongkorn_University "Chulalongkorn University") and [Mahidol University](/wiki/Mahidol_University "Mahidol University").[[306]](#cite_note-316) Thai universities' research output is still relatively low, even though the country's journal publications increased by 20% between 2011 and 2016.[[307]](#cite_note-317) Thailand has the second highest number of English-medium private international schools in [Southeast Asian Nations](/wiki/ASEAN "ASEAN").[[308]](#cite_note-WENR_2018-318) [Cram schools](/wiki/Cram_school "Cram school") are especially popular for university entrance exams.[[309]](#cite_note-319)

Students in ethnic minority areas score consistently lower in standardised national and international tests.[[310]](#cite_note-Draper_2012-320)[[311]](#cite_note-321)[[312]](#cite_note-322) This is likely due to unequal allocation of educational resources, weak teacher training, poverty, and low Thai language skill, the language of the tests.[[310]](#cite_note-Draper_2012-320)[[313]](#cite_note-323)[[314]](#cite_note-324) As of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Thailand&action=edit), Thailand was ranked 89th out of 100 countries globally for English proficiency.[[315]](#cite_note-325) Thailand is the third most popular study destination in ASEAN. The number of international degree students in Thailand increased by 9.7 times between 1999 and 2012, from 1,882 to 20,309 students. Most of international students come from neighbour countries[[308]](#cite_note-WENR_2018-318) like China, Myanmar, Cambodia and Vietnam.[[316]](#cite_note-326)

### Health

Main articles: [Health in Thailand](/wiki/Health_in_Thailand "Health in Thailand"), [Healthcare in Thailand](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Thailand "Healthcare in Thailand"), and [Hospitals in Thailand](/wiki/Hospitals_in_Thailand "Hospitals in Thailand")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Siriraj_Hospital%2CBangkok.jpg/250px-Siriraj_Hospital%2CBangkok.jpg)](/wiki/File:Siriraj_Hospital,Bangkok.jpg)

[Siriraj Hospital](/wiki/Siriraj_Hospital "Siriraj Hospital") in Bangkok, the oldest and largest hospital in Thailand

Thailand ranks world's sixth, and Asia's first in the 2019 [Global Health Security Index](/wiki/Global_Health_Security_Index "Global Health Security Index") of global health security capabilities in 195 countries,[[317]](#cite_note-327) making it the only [developing country](/wiki/Developing_country "Developing country") on the world's top ten. Thailand had 62 hospitals accredited by [Joint Commission International](/wiki/Joint_Commission "Joint Commission").[[318]](#cite_note-328) In 2002, [Bumrungrad](/wiki/Bumrungrad_International_Hospital "Bumrungrad International Hospital") became the first hospital in Asia to meet the standard.[[319]](#cite_note-329)

Health and medical care is overseen by the [Ministry of Public Health](/wiki/Ministry_of_Public_Health_(Thailand) "Ministry of Public Health (Thailand)") (MOPH), with total national expenditures on health amounting to 4.3 per cent of GDP in 2009. [Non-communicable diseases](/wiki/Non-communicable_disease "Non-communicable disease") form the major burden of morbidity and mortality, while infectious diseases including malaria[[320]](#cite_note-330) and tuberculosis,[[321]](#cite_note-331) as well as traffic accidents, are also important public health issues.[[322]](#cite_note-332)

In December 2018, the interim parliament voted to legalise the use of [cannabis](/wiki/Cannabis "Cannabis") for medical reasons, making Thailand the first Southeast Asian country to allow the use of [medical cannabis](/wiki/Medical_cannabis "Medical cannabis").[[323]](#cite_note-333)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Thailand](/wiki/Culture_of_Thailand "Culture of Thailand")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Silk_Loom_Jim_Thompson_House_photo_D_Ramey_Logan.jpg/250px-Silk_Loom_Jim_Thompson_House_photo_D_Ramey_Logan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Silk_Loom_Jim_Thompson_House_photo_D_Ramey_Logan.jpg)

Thai women wearing [sabai](/wiki/Sabai "Sabai"), [Jim Thompson House](/wiki/Jim_Thompson_House "Jim Thompson House")

Thai culture and traditions incorporate influences from India, China, Cambodia, and the rest of Southeast Asia. Thailand's national religion, [Theravada Buddhism](/wiki/Theravada "Theravada"), is central to modern [Thai identity](/wiki/Thai_identity "Thai identity"). [Thai Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Thailand "Buddhism in Thailand") has evolved over millennia from the [Mon kingdom of Dvaravati](/wiki/Dvaravati "Dvaravati") in [Central Thailand](/wiki/Central_Thailand "Central Thailand") (6th–11th century). Nowadays it includes many regional beliefs originating from [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), [animism](/wiki/Animism "Animism"), as well as ancestor worship. The [official calendar](/wiki/Thai_solar_calendar "Thai solar calendar") in Thailand is based on the Eastern version of the [Buddhist Era](/wiki/Buddhist_calendar "Buddhist calendar") (BE). The ancient Thai identity originates from the [Tai-speaking groups](/wiki/Tai_peoples "Tai peoples") who migrated from southern China to Southeast Asia in 6th to 11th century.[[324]](#cite_note-Stentiford-2022-334) The Tai brought the [Thai language](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language") and established city-states.[[324]](#cite_note-Stentiford-2022-334) They mixed with the existing Austro-Asiatic groups like the [Mon](/wiki/Mon_people "Mon people") and [Khmer](/wiki/Khmer_people "Khmer people") while absorbing their cultures and beliefs in the [Chao Phraya Basin](/wiki/Chao_Phraya_River "Chao Phraya River").[[324]](#cite_note-Stentiford-2022-334) The prevalent culture of [Central Thailand](/wiki/Central_Thailand "Central Thailand") formed the basis of the modern Thai identity. This was [spread nationwide](/wiki/Thaification "Thaification") by the [Phibun](/wiki/Plaek_Phibunsongkhram "Plaek Phibunsongkhram") regime to assimilate the ethnic groups in the 1940s.[[325]](#cite_note-335)[[326]](#cite_note-336)[[327]](#cite_note-337)

Several ethnic groups mediated change between their traditional local culture, national Thai, and global cultural influences. [Overseas Chinese](/wiki/Overseas_Chinese "Overseas Chinese") also form a significant part of Thai society, particularly in and around Bangkok. Thai Chinese businesses prosper as part of the larger [bamboo network](/wiki/Bamboo_network "Bamboo network").[[328]](#cite_note-338)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Loi_Krathong_2010_John_Shedrick.jpg/250px-Loi_Krathong_2010_John_Shedrick.jpg)](/wiki/File:Loi_Krathong_2010_John_Shedrick.jpg)

People floating krathong rafts during the [Loi Krathong](/wiki/Loi_Krathong "Loi Krathong") festival in Chiang Mai, Thailand

Respect for elderly and superiors (by age, position, monks, or certain professions) is Thai [mores](/wiki/Mores "Mores"), reflecting in many classes of [honorifics](/wiki/Thai_honorifics "Thai honorifics").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] *[Wai](/wiki/Thai_greeting "Thai greeting")* is a familiar Thai greeting, and is generally offered first by a person who is younger or lower in social status and position.[[329]](#cite_note-Smutkupt-1976-339) Older siblings have duties to younger ones.[[330]](#cite_note-340)

[Taboos](/wiki/Taboo "Taboo") in Thai culture include touching someone's head or pointing with the feet, as the head is considered the most sacred and the foot the lowest part of the body.[[329]](#cite_note-Smutkupt-1976-339)

### Art

Main article: [Thai art](/wiki/Thai_art "Thai art")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/WatPhraKeaw_Ramayana_Chariot.JPG/250px-WatPhraKeaw_Ramayana_Chariot.JPG)](/wiki/File:WatPhraKeaw_Ramayana_Chariot.JPG)

Scene from the [Ramakien](/wiki/Ramakien "Ramakien") depicted on a mural at [Wat Phra Kaew](/wiki/Wat_Phra_Kaew "Wat Phra Kaew")

The origins of Thai art were influenced by [Buddhist art](/wiki/Buddhist_art "Buddhist art") and by scenes from the Indian epics. Traditional Thai [sculpture](/wiki/Sculpture "Sculpture") almost exclusively depicts [images of the Buddha](/wiki/Buddha_image "Buddha image"), being very similar with the other styles from [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia"). Traditional Thai [paintings](/wiki/Painting "Painting") usually consist of book illustrations, and painted ornamentation of buildings such as [palaces](/wiki/Palace "Palace") and [temples](/wiki/Temple "Temple").

Thai art was influenced by indigenous civilisations of the [Mon](/wiki/Dvaravati "Dvaravati") and other civilisations. By the Sukothai and Ayutthaya periods, Thai had developed into its own unique style and was later further influenced by the other Asian styles, mostly by [Sri Lankan](/wiki/Indian_art "Indian art") and [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_art "Chinese art"). Thai sculpture and painting, and the royal courts provided patronage, erecting temples and other religious shrines as acts of merit or to commemorate important events.[[331]](#cite_note-Buddhist_Arts_of_Thailand-341)

Traditional Thai paintings showed subjects in two [dimensions](/wiki/Dimension "Dimension") without [perspective](/wiki/Perspective_(graphical) "Perspective (graphical)"). The size of each element in the picture reflected its degree of importance. The primary technique of [composition](/wiki/Composition_(visual_arts) "Composition (visual arts)") is that of apportioning areas: the main elements are isolated from each other by space transformers. This eliminated the intermediate ground, which would otherwise imply perspective. Perspective was introduced only as a result of [Western](/wiki/Western_world "Western world") influence in the mid-19th century. Monk artist [Khrua In Khong](/wiki/Khrua_In_Khong "Khrua In Khong") is well known as the first artist to introduce [linear perspective](/wiki/Linear_perspective "Linear perspective") to Thai traditional art.[[332]](#cite_note-342)

The most frequent narrative subjects for paintings were or are: the [Jataka](/wiki/Jataka "Jataka") stories, episodes from the life of the [Buddha](/wiki/Gautama_Buddha "Gautama Buddha"), the Buddhist [heavens](/wiki/Heaven "Heaven") and [hells](/wiki/Hell "Hell"), themes derived from the Thai versions of the *Ramayana* and *Mahabharata*, and scenes of daily life. Some of the scenes are influenced by [Thai folklore](/wiki/Thai_folklore "Thai folklore") instead of following strict Buddhist [iconography](/wiki/Iconography "Iconography").[[331]](#cite_note-Buddhist_Arts_of_Thailand-341)

### Architecture

Main article: [Architecture of Thailand](/wiki/Architecture_of_Thailand "Architecture of Thailand")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/The_sculptures_of_two_mythical_giant_demons%2C_Thotsakan_and_Sahatsadecha%2C_guarding_the_eastern_gate_of_the_main_chapel_of_Wat_Arun%2C_Bangkok.jpg/250px-The_sculptures_of_two_mythical_giant_demons%2C_Thotsakan_and_Sahatsadecha%2C_guarding_the_eastern_gate_of_the_main_chapel_of_Wat_Arun%2C_Bangkok.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_sculptures_of_two_mythical_giant_demons,_Thotsakan_and_Sahatsadecha,_guarding_the_eastern_gate_of_the_main_chapel_of_Wat_Arun,_Bangkok.jpg)

Two sculptures in front of the eastern gate to the main chapel of [Wat Arun](/wiki/Wat_Arun "Wat Arun")

The [Ayutthaya Kingdom](/wiki/Ayutthaya_Kingdom "Ayutthaya Kingdom") movement is designed to display might and riches. The temples in Ayutthaya seldom built eaves stretching from the masterhead.[[333]](#cite_note-343) [Buddhist temples in Thailand](/wiki/Buddhist_temples_in_Thailand "Buddhist temples in Thailand") are known as "[wats](/wiki/Wat "Wat")", from the [Pāḷi](/wiki/P%C4%81%E1%B8%B7i "Pāḷi") *vāṭa*, meaning an enclosure: a temple has an enclosing wall that divides it from the secular world. Wat [architecture](/wiki/Architecture "Architecture") demonstrates many differences in layout and style, but they all adhere to the same principles.[[334]](#cite_note-344)

### Literature

Main article: [Thai literature](/wiki/Thai_literature "Thai literature")

Thai literature has had a long history. Even before the establishment of the Sukhothai Kingdom there existed oral and written works.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

During the [Sukhothai Kingdom](/wiki/Sukhothai_Kingdom "Sukhothai Kingdom"), most literary works were written in simple prose with certain alliteration schemes. Major works include King [Ram Khamhaeng Inscription](/wiki/Ram_Khamhaeng_Inscription "Ram Khamhaeng Inscription") describing life at the time, which is considered the first literary work in Thai script, but some historians questioned its authenticity.[[335]](#cite_note-345) *Trai Phum [Phra Ruang](/wiki/Phra_Ruang "Phra Ruang")*, written in 1345 by King [Maha Thammaracha I](/wiki/Maha_Thammaracha_I "Maha Thammaracha I"), expounds Buddhist philosophy based on an extensive study with reference to over 30 sacred texts and could be considered the nation's first piece of research [dissertation](/wiki/Dissertation "Dissertation").[[336]](#cite_note-346)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Sunrise_on_Hat_Saikaew%2C_Koh_Samet.jpg/250px-Sunrise_on_Hat_Saikaew%2C_Koh_Samet.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sunrise_on_Hat_Saikaew,_Koh_Samet.jpg)

Sculptures of [Phra Aphai Mani](/wiki/Phra_Aphai_Mani "Phra Aphai Mani") and the Mermaid from the [epic poem](/wiki/Epic_poem "Epic poem") *[Phra Aphai Mani](/wiki/Phra_Aphai_Mani "Phra Aphai Mani")*, a work of [Sunthorn Phu](/wiki/Sunthorn_Phu "Sunthorn Phu")

During the [Ayutthaya Kingdom](/wiki/Ayutthaya_Kingdom "Ayutthaya Kingdom"), new [poetic](/wiki/Thai_poetry "Thai poetry") forms were created, with different rhyme schemes and metres. It is common to find a combination of different poetic forms in one poetic work. *Lilit [Yuan Phai](/wiki/Yuan_Phai "Yuan Phai")* is a narrative poem describing [the war](/wiki/Ayutthaya%E2%80%93Lan_Na_War_(1441%E2%80%931474) "Ayutthaya–Lan Na War (1441–1474)") between King [Borommatrailokkanat](/wiki/Borommatrailokkanat "Borommatrailokkanat") of Ayutthaya and Prince [Tilokaraj](/wiki/Tilokaraj "Tilokaraj") of [Lan Na](/wiki/Lan_Na "Lan Na"). One literary work is *Kap He Ruea*, composed by Prince [Thammathibet](/wiki/Thammathibet "Thammathibet") in the *[nirat](/wiki/Nirat "Nirat")* tradition. Traditionally, the verse is sung during the [royal barge](/wiki/Royal_barge "Royal barge") procession[[337]](#cite_note-347) and has been the model for subsequent poets to emulate. The same prince also composed the greatly admired *Kap Ho Khlong* on the Visit to Than Thongdaeng and *Kap Ho Khlong Nirat Phrabat*.[[338]](#cite_note-Embassy-348) The [Thonburi](/wiki/Thonburi_Kingdom "Thonburi Kingdom") period produced *[Ramakien](/wiki/Ramakien "Ramakien")*, a verse drama contributed by [King Taksin the Great](/wiki/King_Taksin_the_Great "King Taksin the Great").

During the 18th century [Rattanakosin](/wiki/Rattanakosin_Kingdom "Rattanakosin Kingdom") period, which still fought with the Burmese, many of the early Rattanakosin works dealt with war and military strategy. Some examples are *Nirat Rop Phama Thi Tha Din Daeng*, *Phleng Yao Rop Phama Thi Nakhon Si Thammarat*. There were also verse recitals with musical accompaniment, such as [Mahori](/wiki/Mahori "Mahori") telling the story of *[Kaki](/wiki/Kaki_Klon_Suphap "Kaki Klon Suphap")* and [Sepha](/wiki/Sepha "Sepha"), relating the story of *[Khun Chang Khun Phaen](/wiki/Khun_Chang_Khun_Phaen "Khun Chang Khun Phaen")*. Other recitals include [Sri Thanonchai](/wiki/Sri_Thanonchai "Sri Thanonchai"). The Thai poet [Sunthorn Phu](/wiki/Sunthorn_Phu "Sunthorn Phu") is known as "the bard of Rattanakosin" ([Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): กวีเอกแห่งกรุงรัตนโกสินทร์). Sunthorn Phu is best known for his epic poem *[Phra Aphai Mani](/wiki/Phra_Aphai_Mani "Phra Aphai Mani")*, a versified fantasy-adventure novel, a genre of Siamese literature known as *nithan kham klon* ([Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): นิทานคำกลอน).[[338]](#cite_note-Embassy-348)

Some of the most well-known modern Thai writers include [Kukrit Pramoj](/wiki/Kukrit_Pramoj "Kukrit Pramoj"), [Kulap Saipradit](/wiki/Kulap_Saipradit "Kulap Saipradit"), (penname [Siburapha](/wiki/Siburapha "Siburapha")), Suweeriya Sirisingh (penname Botan), [Chart Korbjitti](/wiki/Chart_Korbjitti "Chart Korbjitti"), [Prabda Yoon](/wiki/Prabda_Yoon "Prabda Yoon"), and [Duanwad Pimwana](/wiki/Duanwad_Pimwana "Duanwad Pimwana").[[339]](#cite_note-349)

### Music and dance

Main articles: [Music of Thailand](/wiki/Music_of_Thailand "Music of Thailand") and [Dance in Thailand](/wiki/Dance_in_Thailand "Dance in Thailand")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Dance_of_the_Ramayana.jpg/250px-Dance_of_the_Ramayana.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dance_of_the_Ramayana.jpg)

[Khon](/wiki/Khon "Khon") show

Aside from folk and regional dances (southern Thailand's [Menora](/wiki/Menora_(dance) "Menora (dance)") and [Ramwong](/wiki/Ramwong "Ramwong"), for example), the two major forms of Thai classical dance drama are [Khon](/wiki/Khon "Khon") and [Lakhon nai](/wiki/Lakhon_nai "Lakhon nai"). In the beginning, both were exclusively court entertainments, and it was not until much later that a popular style of dance theatre, [likay](/wiki/Likay "Likay"), evolved as a diversion for common folk.[[340]](#cite_note-350) Folk dance forms include dance theatre forms like [likay](/wiki/Likay "Likay"), numerous regional dances (*ram*), the ritual dance [ram muay](/wiki/Ram_muay "Ram muay"), and homage to the teacher, [wai khru](/wiki/Wai_khru_ram_muay "Wai khru ram muay").[[341]](#cite_note-351) Both ram muay and wai khru take place before all traditional [muay Thai](/wiki/Muay_Thai "Muay Thai") matches.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The three primary classical ensembles are the [Piphat](/wiki/Piphat "Piphat"), [Khrueang sai](/wiki/Khrueang_sai "Khrueang sai"), and [Mahori](/wiki/Mahori "Mahori"). Mahori employ small ching hand cymbals.[[342]](#cite_note-352)

### Media

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Tony_Jaa_2005.jpg/250px-Tony_Jaa_2005.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tony_Jaa_2005.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/%2817.05.17%29_%EB%B8%94%EB%9E%99%ED%95%91%ED%81%AC_%EB%A6%AC%EC%82%AC_2.jpg/250px-%2817.05.17%29_%EB%B8%94%EB%9E%99%ED%95%91%ED%81%AC_%EB%A6%AC%EC%82%AC_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:(17.05.17)_%EB%B8%94%EB%9E%99%ED%95%91%ED%81%AC_%EB%A6%AC%EC%82%AC_2.jpg)

[Tony Jaa](/wiki/Tony_Jaa "Tony Jaa") and [Lalisa Manobal](/wiki/Lalisa_Manobal "Lalisa Manobal")[[xi]](#cite_note-355)

Main articles: [Mass media in Thailand](/wiki/Mass_media_in_Thailand "Mass media in Thailand"), [Censorship in Thailand](/wiki/Censorship_in_Thailand "Censorship in Thailand"), [Cinema of Thailand](/wiki/Cinema_of_Thailand "Cinema of Thailand"), and [Thai television soap opera](/wiki/Thai_television_soap_opera "Thai television soap opera")

Thai films are exported and exhibited in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia").[[345]](#cite_note-356) Thai cinema has developed its own unique identity.[[346]](#cite_note-357)

The Thai heist thriller film *[Bad Genius](/wiki/Bad_Genius_(2017_film) "Bad Genius (2017 film)")* (2017) was one of the most internationally successful Thai films; it broke Thai film earning records in several Asian countries,[[347]](#cite_note-358) Bad Genius won in 12 categories at the 27th [Suphannahong National Film Awards](/wiki/Suphannahong_National_Film_Awards "Suphannahong National Film Awards"), and also won the Jury Award at the 16th [New York Asian Film Festival](/wiki/New_York_Asian_Film_Festival "New York Asian Film Festival") with a worldwide collection of more than $42 million.[[348]](#cite_note-359)

*[Shutter](/wiki/Shutter_(2004_film) "Shutter (2004 film)")* (2004) was one of the best-known Thai horror movies and was recognised worldwide.[[349]](#cite_note-360) Films such as *[Ong-Bak: Muay Thai Warrior](/wiki/Ong-Bak:_Muay_Thai_Warrior "Ong-Bak: Muay Thai Warrior")* (2003) and *[Tom-Yum-Goong](/wiki/Tom-Yum-Goong "Tom-Yum-Goong")* (2005), starring [Tony Jaa](/wiki/Tony_Jaa "Tony Jaa"), feature distinctive aspects of Thai martial arts "[Muay Thai](/wiki/Muay_Thai "Muay Thai")". Thailand television dramas, known as [Lakorn](/wiki/Thai_television_soap_opera "Thai television soap opera"), have become popular in Thailand and regionally.[[350]](#cite_note-361)

The entertainment industries are estimated to have directly contributed $2.1 billion in GDP to the Thai economy in 2011. They also directly supported 86,600 jobs.[[351]](#cite_note-362) Amongst several [dance-pop](/wiki/Dance-pop "Dance-pop") artists who have made internationally successful are [Lisa](/wiki/Lisa_(rapper) "Lisa (rapper)"),[[352]](#cite_note-363) [Violette Wautier](/wiki/Violette_Wautier "Violette Wautier"),[[353]](#cite_note-364) [Illslick](/wiki/Illslick "Illslick"), and [Tata Young](/wiki/Tata_Young "Tata Young").

### Cuisine

Further information: [Thai cuisine](/wiki/Thai_cuisine "Thai cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Kaeng_matsaman_kai.JPG/250px-Kaeng_matsaman_kai.JPG)](/wiki/File:Kaeng_matsaman_kai.JPG)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Mango_sticy_rice_%283859549574%29.jpg/250px-Mango_sticy_rice_%283859549574%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mango_sticy_rice_(3859549574).jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/TOMYUM.jpg/250px-TOMYUM.jpg)](/wiki/File:TOMYUM.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Phat_Thai_kung_Chang_Khien_street_stall.jpg/250px-Phat_Thai_kung_Chang_Khien_street_stall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Phat_Thai_kung_Chang_Khien_street_stall.jpg)

**Clockwise from top left**: [Chicken massaman curry](/wiki/Massaman_curry "Massaman curry"); [mango sticky rice](/wiki/Mango_sticky_rice "Mango sticky rice"); [pad Thai](/wiki/Pad_Thai "Pad Thai"); and [tom yum](/wiki/Tom_yum "Tom yum")

Thai cuisine is one of the most popular in the world.[[354]](#cite_note-365) Common ingredients include [garlic](/wiki/Garlic "Garlic"), [lemongrass](/wiki/Lemongrass "Lemongrass"), [kaffir lime](/wiki/Kaffir_lime "Kaffir lime"), [galangal](/wiki/Galangal "Galangal"), [turmeric](/wiki/Turmeric "Turmeric"), [coriander](/wiki/Coriander "Coriander"), and [coconut milk](/wiki/Coconut_milk "Coconut milk").[[355]](#cite_note-366)[[356]](#cite_note-367)[[357]](#cite_note-368)[[358]](#cite_note-369)[[359]](#cite_note-370) Each region of Thailand has its specialities: *kaeng khiao wan* ([green curry](/wiki/Green_curry "Green curry")) in the central region, *som tam* ([green papaya salad](/wiki/Green_papaya_salad "Green papaya salad")) in the northeast, *[khao soi](/wiki/Khao_soi "Khao soi")* in the north, and [*massaman* curry](/wiki/Massaman_curry "Massaman curry") in the south.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2017, seven Thai dishes appeared on a list of the "World's 50 Best Foods"—an online worldwide poll by [CNN Travel](/wiki/CNN_Travel "CNN Travel"). Thailand had more dishes on the list than any other country. They were: *[tom yam](/wiki/Tom_yam "Tom yam") goong* (4th), *[pad Thai](/wiki/Pad_Thai "Pad Thai")* (5th), *som tam* (6th), *massaman* curry (10th), [green curry](/wiki/Green_curry "Green curry") (19th), [Thai fried rice](/wiki/Thai_fried_rice "Thai fried rice") (24th) and *[nam tok mu](/wiki/Nam_tok_mu "Nam tok mu")* (36th).[[360]](#cite_note-371) Two desserts were also listed in CNN's 50 Best Desserts Around The World: [mango sticky rice](/wiki/Mango_sticky_rice "Mango sticky rice") and [tub tim krob](/wiki/Tub_tim_krob "Tub tim krob").[[361]](#cite_note-372)

The staple food in Thailand is rice, particularly [jasmine rice](/wiki/Jasmine_rice "Jasmine rice"), which forms part of almost every meal. Thailand is a leading exporter of rice, and Thais consume over 100 kg of milled rice per person per year.[[362]](#cite_note-373) Thailand is also the world leader in edible insect industry[[363]](#cite_note-374) and well known for its street food; Bangkok is sometimes called the street food capital of the world.[[364]](#cite_note-375)[[365]](#cite_note-376)

### Units of measurement

Further information: [Thai units of measurement](/wiki/Thai_units_of_measurement "Thai units of measurement")

Thailand generally uses the [metric system](/wiki/Metric_system "Metric system"), but [traditional units of measurement](/wiki/Thai_units_of_measurement "Thai units of measurement") for land area are used, and [imperial units](/wiki/Imperial_units "Imperial units") of measurement are occasionally used for building materials. Years are numbered as B.E. ([Buddhist Era](/wiki/Thai_solar_calendar "Thai solar calendar")) in educational settings, civil service, government, contracts, and newspaper datelines. However, in banking, and increasingly in industry and commerce, standard Western year (Christian or [Common Era](/wiki/Common_Era "Common Era")) counting is the standard practice.[[366]](#cite_note-377)

### Sports

Main article: [Sport in Thailand](/wiki/Sport_in_Thailand "Sport in Thailand")

See also: [Thailand at the Olympics](/wiki/Thailand_at_the_Olympics "Thailand at the Olympics"), [Rugby union in Thailand](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Thailand "Rugby union in Thailand"), [Golf in Thailand](/wiki/Golf_in_Thailand "Golf in Thailand"), [Football in Thailand](/wiki/Football_in_Thailand "Football in Thailand"), and [List of sporting events held in Thailand](/wiki/List_of_sporting_events_held_in_Thailand "List of sporting events held in Thailand")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Muay_Thai_Fight_Us_Vs_Burma_%2880668065%29.jpeg/250px-Muay_Thai_Fight_Us_Vs_Burma_%2880668065%29.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Muay_Thai_Fight_Us_Vs_Burma_(80668065).jpeg)

[Muay Thai](/wiki/Muay_Thai "Muay Thai"), Thailand's signature sport

[Muay Thai](/wiki/Muay_Thai "Muay Thai") (lit. 'Thai boxing') is a [combat sport](/wiki/Combat_sport "Combat sport") that uses stand-up striking along with various [clinching](/wiki/Clinch_fighting "Clinch fighting") techniques. Muay Thai became widespread internationally in the late-20th to 21st centuries. Famous practitioners include [Buakaw Banchamek](/wiki/Buakaw_Banchamek "Buakaw Banchamek"), [Samart Payakaroon](/wiki/Samart_Payakaroon "Samart Payakaroon"), and [Apidej Sit-Hirun](/wiki/Apidej_Sit-Hirun "Apidej Sit-Hirun").[[367]](#cite_note-378) [Association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") has overtaken Muay Thai as the most widely followed sport in Thailand. The [Thailand national football team](/wiki/Thailand_national_football_team "Thailand national football team") has played the [AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/AFC_Asian_Cup "AFC Asian Cup") six times and reached the semifinals in 1972. The country has hosted the Asian Cup twice, in [1972](/wiki/1972_AFC_Asian_Cup "1972 AFC Asian Cup")[[368]](#cite_note-379) and in [2007](/wiki/2007_AFC_Asian_Cup "2007 AFC Asian Cup") (along with Indonesia, Malaysia, and Vietnam for the 2007).

[Volleyball](/wiki/Volleyball "Volleyball") is rapidly growing as one of the most popular sports. The [women's team](/wiki/Thailand_women%27s_national_volleyball_team "Thailand women's national volleyball team") has often participated in the [World Championship](/wiki/FIVB_Volleyball_Women%27s_World_Championship "FIVB Volleyball Women's World Championship"), [World Cup](/wiki/FIVB_Volleyball_Women%27s_World_Cup "FIVB Volleyball Women's World Cup"), and [World Grand Prix](/wiki/FIVB_Volleyball_World_Grand_Prix "FIVB Volleyball World Grand Prix") [Asian Championship](/wiki/Asian_Championship "Asian Championship"). They have won the [Asian Championship](/wiki/Asian_Women%27s_Volleyball_Championship "Asian Women's Volleyball Championship") twice and the [Asian Cup](/wiki/AVC_Cup_for_Women "AVC Cup for Women") once. [Takraw](/wiki/Takraw "Takraw") is a sport native to Thailand in which the players hit a rattan ball and are only allowed to use their feet, knees, chest, and head to touch the ball. [Sepak takraw](/wiki/Sepak_takraw "Sepak takraw") is a form of this sport which is similar to volleyball. A rather similar game but played only with the feet is [buka ball](/wiki/Buka_ball "Buka ball").

[Rugby](/wiki/Rugby_football "Rugby football") is also a growing sport in Thailand with the [Thailand national rugby union team](/wiki/Thailand_national_rugby_union_team "Thailand national rugby union team") rising to be ranked 61st in the world.[[369]](#cite_note-380) Thailand became the first country in the world to host an international 80 welterweight rugby tournament in 2005.[[370]](#cite_note-381) Thailand has also attracts golfers from Japan, Korea, and Western countries.[[371]](#cite_note-382) There are more than 200 world-class golf courses nationwide.[[372]](#cite_note-383) For basketball, the Chang Thailand Slammers won the 2011 [ASEAN Basketball League](/wiki/ASEAN_Basketball_League "ASEAN Basketball League") Championship.[[373]](#cite_note-384) The [Thailand national basketball team](/wiki/Thailand_national_basketball_team "Thailand national basketball team") had its most successful year at the [1966 Asian Games](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_1966_Asian_Games "Basketball at the 1966 Asian Games") where it won the silver medal.[[374]](#cite_note-385)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Bangkok_Lumpinee_Boxing_Stadium_1.jpg/250px-Bangkok_Lumpinee_Boxing_Stadium_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bangkok_Lumpinee_Boxing_Stadium_1.jpg)

[Lumpinee Boxing Stadium](/wiki/Lumpinee_Boxing_Stadium "Lumpinee Boxing Stadium")

The [Lumpinee Boxing Stadium](/wiki/Lumpinee_Boxing_Stadium "Lumpinee Boxing Stadium") originally sited at [Rama IV Road](/wiki/Rama_IV_Road "Rama IV Road") near [Lumphini Park](/wiki/Lumphini_Park "Lumphini Park") hosted its final Muay Thai boxing matches on 8 February 2014 after the venue first opened in December 1956. On 11 February 2014, the stadium was relocated to Ram Intra Road due to the new venue's capacity.[[375]](#cite_note-386) [Thammasat Stadium](/wiki/Thammasat_Stadium "Thammasat Stadium") in Bangkok was built for the [1998 Asian Games](/wiki/1998_Asian_Games "1998 Asian Games"). [Rajamangala National Stadium](/wiki/Rajamangala_National_Stadium "Rajamangala National Stadium") is the biggest sporting arena in Thailand, with a capacity of around 50,000.[[376]](#cite_note-387)
